posts.summary: The post argues that many internet platforms like Facebook function as public goods but are forced into traditional for-profit business models that misalign with their societal value. Using Facebook as a central example, Asparouhova asks what would have happened if it had followed Wikipedia's nonprofit path instead of monetizing through advertising and data collection. She observes that audience-based companies—those delivering massive value through connection and communication—didn't exist before the internet and were retroactively shoehorned into existing business frameworks. The post surveys emerging funding vehicles like AngelList Syndicates, crowdfunding platforms, and nonprofit-friendly accelerators as potential alternatives. Ultimately, she argues that funders hold the power to incentivize impact over valuations, and that the online world will eventually develop nonprofit structures parallel to those in the offline world.
key_points: Audience-based platforms like Facebook and Twitter function as public goods—nonexcludable and nonrivalrous—yet are forced into for-profit monetization models like advertising
Wikipedia's nonprofit model demonstrates a viable alternative path that preserves neutrality and user privacy, yet is treated as an anomaly rather than a template
The traditional vertical value chain is breaking down into horizontal, curated marketplaces, but our funding and business frameworks haven't kept pace
New funding vehicles (AngelList Syndicates, crowdfunding equity, nonprofit accelerator investments) are emerging that could support impact-focused rather than profit-focused companies
The responsibility for change lies with funders who write the checks, not with startups who are rationally responding to existing incentives
money_quotes: Customers won't pay because they're essentially public goods: goods that are nonexcludable (if they want to reach scale, they can't prevent people from using it), and nonrivalrous (one person's use of Facebook doesn't diminish another person's ability to use it).
What if tech were valued in our society because it helps us build useful things for the world on the cheap, not because of its inflated valuations and perceived glamorous lifestyle?
What if Facebook had become a nonprofit because they believed it was more important to create a neutral space for communication and protect their users' privacy than to IPO?
In the offline world, not everything that provides value is a for-profit corporation. The online world, as it matures, will eventually follow suit.

This type of company didn't exist before the Internet, and arguably, not before 2002, with the launch of Friendster. We didn't have a set of rules for them yet, so we backdated them into our existing business rules.
It would be naive to assume that startups will learn to do the 'right thing' and not pursue their dreams of mega-billion unicorns. They're currently not being incentivized to do otherwise.

It's up to funders - those who are writing the checks - to take a step back and think about what kind of world they want to create, and how they can use their dollars to influence what that world will look like.
posts.key_insight: Internet platforms that function as public goods need funding models designed for impact rather than profit, and the responsibility for creating those models lies with funders, not founders.

posts.summary: The post argues that Silicon Valley's reputation for anti-materialism is a myth. Rather than rejecting materialism, tech culture has simply replaced traditional status symbols like luxury cars and designer goods with a new form of conspicuous consumption: on-demand services. Wealth is displayed not through possessions but through the ability to outsource every task and reclaim time. This creates a blurred class dynamic where service providers and consumers are demographically indistinguishable, masking the capitalist nature of these relationships. The author concludes that tech workers are just as dependent on their consumption habits as any traditional power elite.
key_points: Silicon Valley has replaced traditional materialism with service-based consumption, where wealth is measured by what you can hire others to do rather than what you own
Time is the ultimate status symbol in tech culture, and the wealthiest demonstrate their status by outsourcing mundane tasks through on-demand services
The gig economy blurs class lines by making service providers and consumers demographically similar, obscuring power dynamics
The few physical goods tech workers do purchase are grossly overpriced status markers like $5 coffee and $4 toast
Despite idealistic rhetoric about impact, Silicon Valley benefits from wealth no differently than Wall Street
money_quotes: Silicon Valley types are characterized as people who care about not caring.
Your wealth isn't measured by what you own, but rather what you can hire other people to do for you.
Time is the most valuable thing in Silicon Valley. And the wealthiest can afford to capture as much of that time as possible.
Are you a Lyft driver, or are you someone who takes Lyfts?
Hiring people to do things for you is the very definition of capitalism. It's just not as obvious when the person you're hiring might be someone you meet at a party later that night.
Silicon Valley types are just as dependent on services to survive as the power exec of the 90s might have been dependent on his or her 6-car garage and Lamborghini.
Even if we came here for the impact, we're staying for the money - and benefiting from it no differently than a Wall Street banker.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley hasn't rejected materialism but reinvented it, replacing conspicuous ownership with conspicuous outsourcing of labor, while demographic similarities between service providers and consumers obscure the underlying class dynamics.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that 'doing good' should not be confined to traditional nonprofit structures, B Corps, or explicit social causes. She contends that innovation and social impact can come from any corporate form, and that evaluating companies should focus on their behavior and mission rather than their tax designation. Using examples like Larry Page's preference for funding Elon Musk over charity, and nonprofits that shifted from low-risk programs to tackling systemic problems, she makes the case that the social sector needs more risk-taking and innovation. She concludes that tech companies without clear revenue models should be able to explore alternative funding structures rather than being forced into profit maximization.
key_points: B Corps have advanced the conversation about corporate purpose but risk creating an exclusive label that alienates companies doing good in unconventional ways
Larry Page's preference for funding Musk over charity reflects a lack of faith in the social sector's ability to innovate, not a lack of interest in impact

Tax status should be determined by the economics of a business model, not by whether the cause is perceived as 'social' — companies become nonprofits when profit-seeking would compromise their mission

Tech companies providing clear social value without clear revenue models (like Snapchat) shouldn't be forced into profit maximization simply because they solve communication rather than homelessness problems
The tech sector should support multiple funding and organizational models for delivering on mission, just as the social sector already does
money_quotes: Labels like these run the risk of alienating rather than normalizing the idea that companies doing good come in all shapes and sizes.
"Doing good" does not just mean charity, philanthropy, or helping those less fortunate than you. "Doing good" means changing the game - no matter the industry.

We need to uncouple the idea of doing good with labels like for-profits, non-profits, social causes, or B Corps, and instead focus on why people are doing what they do, and how they go about doing it.
Are they preserving the status quo, or solving big, hairy problems? Are they excited to make an impact on their society, or are they in it for recognition or money?
Companies become nonprofits when being profit-driven might compromise the value and integrity of the service they provide.
Your tax status isn't determined by the cause you tackle. It's based on the economics of your model.

What should we do with tech companies like Snapchat who are clearly innovating and providing value, but don't have a clear revenue model? Why do we assume those companies need to make as much money as possible?
It's time for the tech sector to consider supporting multiple ways to deliver on mission as well.
posts.key_insight: A company's social value should be judged by the significance of the problems it tackles and its commitment to mission, not by whether it carries a nonprofit label or pursues an explicitly charitable cause.

posts.summary: The post draws parallels between philanthropic innovation funding and venture capital, arguing both are high-risk, high-return investment strategies differing only in whether they seek social or financial returns. Asparouhova suggests philanthropic funders should adopt VC principles like portfolio thinking, patience for nonlinear results, and willingness to swing for the fences. Conversely, she argues VCs should learn from philanthropy by recognizing externalities their portfolio companies create and embracing the social value embedded in their investments. The essay frames these two worlds as more similar than either side typically acknowledges, using examples like malaria-resistant mosquitoes versus mosquito nets to illustrate the risk-return tradeoff. She concludes that the ecosystems built to effect change are fundamentally similar regardless of sector.
key_points: Philanthropic innovation funding and venture capital are structurally identical strategies differing only in whether returns are social or financial
Philanthropy's expectation that every individual grant show evidence of success leads to underinvestment in transformative but risky ideas
VCs should recognize the externalities their portfolio companies create, understanding that impact extends beyond individual company performance into broader ecosystems
Both sectors would benefit from adopting a 'double bottom line' that acknowledges financial and social returns coexist in any investment
money_quotes: Innovation funding and venture capital differ only in that the former is aimed at social returns, the latter on financial returns.
Someone's promise to deliver 100 mosquito nets is probably more feasible than someone's promise to breed malaria-resistant mosquitos, but if the latter works, the impact would be enormous.
In the social sector, funders expect that each of their investments can show evidence of success. This expectation leads to investing in lower-risk, but smaller opportunities.
VCs understand that investing in a lot of medium-return companies might be a safer bet, but would totally defeat the purpose of venture capital.
Funders in the social sector recognize that change happens in an ecosystem, not a silo.

If you give free shoes to a village, that might put the local cobbler out of business. It might also make it easier for a child to walk to school, so that you're not just affecting her clothing, but her education.
The parallels that can be drawn between innovation funding and venture capital serve as a reminder that the ecosystems we create to effect change are similar, no matter the sector they tackle.
posts.key_insight: Philanthropic funders and venture capitalists operate fundamentally similar investment models and would both improve by adopting each other's best practices—philanthropy needs VC's tolerance for failure and patience, while VC needs philanthropy's awareness of externalities and social value.

posts.summary: The post draws a parallel between offline government services (street lights, police, waste management) and online services (Google, Facebook, WhatsApp) that form the backbone of internet communication. Asparouhova argues that a handful of corporations have become de facto governments of the internet by housing these essential 'public utilities.' She reframes large tech acquisitions not as soft landings but as a form of centralized sponsorship of public services. However, she notes the fundamental tension: corporations are legally obligated to serve shareholders, not the public, making them structurally incapable of being neutral governing bodies. She raises the question of whether the next wave of ephemeral and anonymous apps will follow the same corporate acquisition pattern or inspire a different model for sustaining public internet infrastructure.
key_points: Internet platforms like Google and Facebook function as public utilities analogous to offline government services, forming the backbone of online communication
High-value tech acquisitions can be reframed as corporate sponsorship of public services that couldn't sustain themselves financially
For-profit corporations are structurally incapable of serving as neutral governing bodies because their legal obligation is to shareholders
The rise of ephemeral and anonymous apps that don't collect user data threatens the advertising-based model that funds these 'public services'
The question of how to sustain essential internet infrastructure without corporate acquisition remains unresolved
money_quotes: Most of these 'public services of the Internet' are housed by just a couple of corporations.
If government's role is, at least in part, to support the functional backbone services of our society, these corporations have become our de-facto governments.
These companies were doing just fine in terms of audience and reach, but they never really figured out the revenue-generating piece of their business.
A corporation's job is to deliver value to their shareholders, not to organize the people.
Companies like Google and Facebook might serve the same purpose online as government entities offline, but they are not - cannot be - a neutral entity. Even if they wanted to be.
It shouldn't ever be a for-profit corporation's job to serve the role of public government.
Is it going to be just another corporate acquisition? Or can we envision something different?
posts.key_insight: Major tech corporations have assumed the role of internet governments by housing essential public services, but their for-profit structure makes them fundamentally incapable of serving as neutral governing bodies.

posts.summary: The post uses Google Flu Trends as a cautionary tale about the dangers of making predictions when the underlying measurement tools and landscape are constantly changing. GFT's algorithm failed because it depended on Google Search behavior remaining stable, which it didn't. The author extends this lesson to the startup world, arguing that data-driven predictions about startup success are similarly unreliable because the internet, fundraising norms, founder archetypes, and success metrics all evolve rapidly. She also highlights the lack of accountability for failed predictions, since people forget unsuccessful startups quickly. The core argument is that in fast-moving industries, historical data is an unreliable guide to the future.
key_points: Google Flu Trends failed because its algorithm depended on Google Search remaining stable, but search algorithms and user behavior changed significantly over time
Startup success criteria are volatile — what makes a good founder, how companies raise money, and which metrics matter all shift rapidly
There is a dangerous lack of accountability for data-driven predictions, since failed startups are forgotten quickly while lucky successes are rationalized after the fact
The 'next big thing' almost by definition defies existing predictive models, making historical pattern-matching unreliable
Metrics themselves are unstable — social metrics didn't exist a decade ago, and companies can now be worth billions with zero revenue
money_quotes: We don't know what will make the next startup successful based on what's worked in the past, because the standards for success change so rapidly.
Almost by definition, the 'next big thing' is going to be something we aren't prepared for.
We don't know what the best metrics are to measure the next generation of startups, because we don't yet know how they will be measured.
Revenue probably once sounded like a reliable metric 20 years ago, but now, it's possible for a company to have zero revenue and be valued at over $1B.
If a startup defies the algorithm, we just say they got lucky.
The ground is moving beneath our feet even as we try to build upon it.
Perhaps nowhere else are algorithms for success more volatile than in technology-based startups.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven predictions break down in rapidly evolving industries because the underlying landscape, measurement tools, and success criteria are all shifting simultaneously, yet there is little accountability when those predictions fail.

posts.summary: The post argues that while cultural critics have focused on technology addiction as the defining weakness of modern society, a subtler problem is emerging: the internet's capacity to make empathy selfish and performative. Using examples like Kony 2012 and a viral poverty essay where supporters didn't care about factual accuracy, Asparouhova suggests that online empathy may be 'masturbatory'—a reflexive act of emotional self-gratification rather than genuine concern for others. She poses a thought experiment about whether people would still use Reddit if all content were computer-generated, suggesting the answer reveals how disconnected online engagement is from reality. The post concludes that the internet has amplified an age-old philosophical question about whether altruism is truly selfless, and predicts this will be the next great subject for cultural satirists.
key_points: Cultural critiques of technology addiction (screen time, digital detox) are already becoming dated; deeper structural problems are emerging
The internet enables performative empathy where people engage emotionally without caring whether the content is real, as shown by Kony 2012 and viral fundraising fraud
Online empathy may be fundamentally selfish—a controlled, curated emotional experience that substitutes for engagement with the physical world
The internet lets people curate their emotional lives, choosing exactly what to feel outraged or joyful about, reducing empathy to consumption
The philosophical question of whether altruism is selfish has been amplified by the internet to a societal scale
money_quotes: As we become more empathetic to what's on our screens, we risk becoming less empathetic to the physical world around us.

It begs the question whether Internet empathy is simply masturbatory: a reflexive search to relieve our desires for joy and anger and emotion through external stimuli that could just as easily be fake as it is real.
But maybe we don't care, because we want to believe that it all matters.
The empathy that the internet has created may be purely selfish, after all.
Fiction or non-fiction, it felt good then and it feels good now.
We can limit our outrage to videos from Upworthy that match our political biases. We can follow @EmergencyPuppy on Twitter to give ourselves a daily dose of joy.
The internet has amplified that, made it harder to know when we're caring about others or just caring about ourselves.
posts.key_insight: The internet's greatest cultural danger isn't technology addiction but the way it transforms empathy into a curated, self-serving emotional experience detached from reality.

posts.summary: The post challenges the notion that technological progress should be accepted uncritically, arguing that humanity's primary goal is survival rather than innovation for its own sake. Asparouhova draws on two historical examples—the international ban on chemical weapons after WWI and the backlash against processed food starting in the 1980s—to show that societies have repeatedly chosen to step back from technological capabilities when the costs became clear. She then draws a parallel to the early 2010s embrace of big data and targeted advertising, suggesting we are still in an 'infatuation phase' with data-driven technology. The post concludes that while it may be too early for a full reckoning with big data's societal costs, we should remember that past technological enthusiasm has consistently required recalibration.
key_points: Technological progress is inevitable but not humanity's sole purpose; survival requires balancing innovation against moral and physical limitations.
The chemical weapons taboo and the retreat from processed food demonstrate that societies have intentionally stepped back from available technologies when harms became apparent.
The early 2010s infatuation with big data and targeted advertising mirrors past phases of uncritical technological enthusiasm.
Even the NSA surveillance scandal failed to spark meaningful public pushback, suggesting society may still be in the 'infatuation phase' with data-driven technology.
Historical precedent suggests a future recalibration around data and privacy is likely, even if the timing remains uncertain.
money_quotes: It's true that progress is inevitable, but advancing technology is not the sole goal for humanity.

Technological innovation must be weighed against consideration of our moral and physical limitations. Only the former keeps our numbers growing; only the latter keeps us from destroying ourselves too quickly.
We had the technology to mutilate our enemies, but we decided not to.
We had the technology to do away with food, but we decided not to.
Maybe we're still in the infatuation phase, basking in the glow of a screen that knows exactly who we are and what we want, food scientists gazing blissfully upon our glorious, jiggling Jell-O molds.
Maybe, as with chemical warfare and food processing, we curious humans have to make mistakes for a couple more decades first.
Technological innovation for its own sake is meaningless without context.
posts.key_insight: History shows that societies routinely embrace new technologies with uncritical enthusiasm before eventually stepping back to recalibrate, and our current infatuation with big data and surveillance is likely no different.

posts.summary: This post presents a detailed data analysis of Product Hunt's categories and contributors, based on the author manually tagging 1,023 products. The analysis finds that a contributor's reputation (internal or external) doesn't correlate with a product's popularity — what matters is the product itself. Developer Tools, Design, and Product Updates top the category list, reflecting the hacker/designer makeup of the community. The author argues that Product Hunt is fundamentally different from startup databases because many posted products are fun utilities or side projects, not venture-backed companies. The piece concludes that Product Hunt is best understood as a place for product lovers, not just startup scouts.
key_points: A contributor's posting frequency or Twitter following has no correlation with how well their product performs on Product Hunt — popularity is driven by the product itself
The top categories (Developer Tools, Design, Product Updates) reflect that Product Hunt's community is primarily hackers and designers sharing things they personally find useful
91% of Product Hunt contributors are male, revealing a significant gender disparity in who curates the platform
Product Hunt serves a dual function: discovering new products and tracking updates to existing ones, which was an unexpected finding
Unlike AngelList or Crunchbase, many Product Hunt submissions are fun side projects or utilities rather than venture-scale startups
money_quotes: I realized picking just one category per product was more art than science, and I didn't feel comfortable outsourcing.
In other words, it's possible to post something just once that turns out to be popular. How well the product is received probably has more to do with the product itself.
Not everything posted to Product Hunt is meant to be a startup.
I doubt the creators of @pmarca-tweet-essays or Emoji Zone are trying to raise a $1M seed round for their ideas. They're just fun, quirky, wonderful places on the internet.
There is joy to be found in opening up Product Hunt and finding yourself unexpectedly wandering through the solar system or experiencing a light year.
Product Hunt overlaps with the world of startup databases and sourcing tools in a big way, but we shouldn't forget that at its core, it's a place for product lovers.
posts.key_insight: Product Hunt is fundamentally a community for product lovers discovering useful and delightful things, not merely a startup sourcing tool, and a product's reception depends on its own merits rather than the reputation of whoever posts it.

posts.summary: The post argues that modern entrepreneurs have become this generation's most influential artists, using the Yo app's controversial $1M raise as a jumping-off point. Asparouhova contends that startups have shifted from being purely about technical ability or business acumen to being vehicles for creative expression. She draws parallels between Silicon Valley founders and historical artistic movements, comparing the public debate over Yo to reactions to Dadaism's avant-garde provocations. The piece suggests that lowered barriers to software development have attracted creative minds to tech, and that the cultural impact of startups is being overlooked amid bubble speculation.
key_points: Startups have evolved from technical/business ventures into forms of creative output, blurring the line between entrepreneurship and art
Lower barriers to software development have attracted creative minds to tech, making code as accessible as a paintbrush
The post-2008 recession appeal of entrepreneurship lies in creative freedom and societal influence, not just economic opportunity
The public debate over apps like Yo mirrors historical reactions to avant-garde art movements like Dadaism
Silicon Valley's cultural significance is being overlooked in favor of narrow debates about valuations and bubbles
money_quotes: Somewhere between Google and Yo, startups, and our speculation around them, became less about technical ability or business acumen and more about creative output.
Collectively, we're captivated by the potential of technology, a place to let one's imagination run wild and envision the world as it should be rather than as it is.

The exploding appeal of entrepreneurship in the wake of the 2008 recession lies in the relief: a place to think creatively, live freely, and influence society…not to mention a semblance of a real job to write home about.

The heated debate over whether Yo is a joke or the next billion-dollar idea is not unlike the public reaction to post-WWI Dadaism, whose avant-garde "anti-art" approach challenged what we considered to be an acceptable art form.
We're so focused on making history for technology that we forget we're making history for culture, too.
Is Yo worth a $1M investment? I don't know. Was Marcel Duchamp's "Fountain" worth $1.7M?
posts.key_insight: The startup ecosystem has become a cultural and artistic movement in its own right, with entrepreneurs serving as this generation's avant-garde artists pushing boundaries and shaping public discourse.

posts.summary: The post examines how peer-to-peer platforms behave at scale, arguing that pure P2P models cannot own their supply or demand because participants freely move between competing platforms. Using examples like Udemy/Skillshare and Uber/Lyft, Asparouhova shows how P2P competitors become indistinguishable as they grow. She concludes that successful P2P models may need to be monopolies in their markets, functioning more like governments overseeing human behavior than traditional corporations. The post raises open questions about whether these models should be public corporations, private companies, or nonprofits, and how financing should adapt.
key_points: Pure peer-to-peer platforms cannot own their supply or demand, as both sides freely participate across competing platforms
P2P companies that start with distinct identities become indistinguishable from competitors at scale, as seen with Uber and Lyft
Successful pure P2P models may need to be monopolies, functioning as conduits or governance structures rather than traditional corporations
Competing against a P2P monopoly requires dismantling it piece-by-piece rather than head-on competition
The corporate structure and financing models for P2P businesses remain unresolved—public corporation, private company, or nonprofit could each be appropriate
money_quotes: No peer-to-peer online learning platform can own its supply or demand, which has made it difficult for a clear winner to emerge at scale.
It's great for those of us who participate on these platforms, because it means the company's value is concentrated in the people's, not the corporation's, hands.
If any one of these peer-to-peer companies were to shut down today, we'd just use the next-available platform.
Successful pure peer-to-peer models have to be monopolies in their respective markets, or else they will be indistinguishable from their competitors at scale.
Rather than corporations, perhaps these peer-to-peer models are conduits, not dissimilar to governments, overseeing a certain class of human behavior.
To compete with a peer-to-peer monopoly, new companies entering the market would need to dismantle it piece-by-piece rather than compete with it head on.
posts.key_insight: Pure peer-to-peer platforms cannot own their supply or demand, so they must either achieve monopoly status or become indistinguishable from competitors—making them more like governing conduits than traditional corporations.

posts.summary: The post identifies a pattern where companies fund experimental or hard-to-monetize products through high-margin B2B contracts or ancillary businesses. Examples include B2C companies subsidized by B2B work, hardware companies funding R&D through software licensing, and Duolingo keeping its app free by selling crowd-sourced translations. The author draws a parallel to Amazon's strategy of offering unprofitable features to capture market share. She raises the question of whether this cross-subsidization model could fund socially valuable but hard-to-monetize products like ad-free social networks or journalism.
key_points: Several startups fund experimental consumer products through reliable, high-margin B2B contracts or adjacent business lines
Decoupling a product from its own profitability opens up the possibility of funding socially valuable but hard-to-monetize services
Duolingo exemplifies this model by selling crowd-sourced translations to keep its language-learning app free without resorting to ads
Public demand for ad-free social networks (App.net, Diaspora, Ello) demonstrates a market need that acquisitions address imperfectly
money_quotes: Basically, use a reliable and lucrative high-margin business to fund an experimental, lower-margin part of their business.
Decoupling products from their profitability offers other interesting implications.
What if a company used high-margin parts of their business to fund harder-to-ethically-monetize businesses like social networks, media companies, or journalism?
One wonders how they will produce an outsized return to investors without advertising.
Acquisitions can also lead to more conflicts of interest.
I'm curious whether more companies use this business model to fund products that are valuable to society, but are hard to monetize.
posts.key_insight: Companies can use profitable business lines to cross-subsidize socially valuable products that are difficult to monetize directly, offering an alternative to ad-supported models.

posts.summary: The post argues that while transparency is valuable for organizations and society, privacy is equally essential and should not be diminished in the pursuit of openness. Written in the context of Buffer's transparent Series A announcement, Asparouhova identifies four key areas where privacy provides irreplaceable value: creativity, moral development, intimacy, and authenticity. She contends that people are creatively inhibited when watched, need private space to develop moral character through experimentation, derive intimacy from the contrast between public and private selves, and lose authenticity when everything is visible. The post serves as a counterweight to the tech industry's growing enthusiasm for radical transparency.
key_points: Research shows people become creatively inhibited when they feel watched or judged, which is why brainstorming experts recommend starting with designated alone time
Privacy provides a safe space for moral experimentation—learning consequences through personal experience builds character in ways that secondhand knowledge cannot
Intimacy depends on the contrast between public and private selves; full transparency would eliminate the crossover from stranger to intimate partner that creates connection
Without privacy, society risks becoming an echo chamber of public-facing selves—false positivity without authentic engagement with fear, shame, or guilt
money_quotes: I truly believe that transparency can offer unlimited benefits to society, as long as it does not diminish the value of privacy.
As we continue to debate transparency in the coming months and years, I hope we remember to root our actions in the things we value, rather than in their absence.
Creativity requires not being afraid to look silly, and we don't want to look silly in front of other people.
Privacy gives us a safe space to conduct moral experiments.
By learning what we can or cannot get away with in life, we understand the consequences of our actions, which become defining experiences for our character.
So much of who we are as humans stems from the contrast between our private and public selves.

Without privacy, we lose a sense of authenticity, and society risks becoming an echo chamber of our public selves - false positivity, cheering one another on, without ever getting real about the things we're afraid of, ashamed of, or feeling guilty about.
posts.key_insight: Privacy is not the enemy of transparency but its essential complement—it enables creativity, moral growth, intimacy, and authenticity that a fully transparent society would destroy.

posts.summary: The post draws a parallel between baby-naming trends from Freakonomics and gendered career patterns, observing that women disproportionately pursue graduate school while men gravitate toward tech and entrepreneurship. Asparouhova notes that graduate enrollment is majority female and declining overall, and recalls that computer programming was once considered women's work until it gained prestige. She hypothesizes that women have never been collectively represented in careers considered powerful by their contemporaries—they arrive only after the status has shifted. She speculates that women will eventually dominate tech founding, but only once it loses its cultural cachet. The post concludes by advising career-minded people to stay ahead of trends rather than follow current prestige signals.
key_points: 58% of first-time graduate students are women, and overall graduate enrollment has been declining—suggesting a gendered divergence in career strategy
The Freakonomics baby-naming pattern (high-status trends trickle down, then lose prestige) may apply to career choices between genders
Programming was once considered women's work; men entered the field as it gained social cachet, displacing women
Women may never have been collectively represented in careers considered powerful at the time they held them
The strategic takeaway is to stay one step ahead of prestige trends rather than following current ones
money_quotes: Does the same thing happen between men and women with their careers?
Maybe graduate school was the thing to do until recently, but now men have turned their eye toward pursuing careers in tech and entrepreneurship, leaving women behind.
Women have simply never been collectively represented in a position considered to be respected and powerful by their contemporaries.
30 years from now, I can confidently predict there will be plenty of women who are tech founders, even if we cease all gender equality efforts right now.
But did that mean we succeeded, if being a tech founder is no longer en vogue?
Rather than focus on getting a graduate degree, learning to code, or starting a company, the goal should be to stay ahead of the trends.
posts.key_insight: Women may consistently arrive in prestigious career fields only after the prestige has migrated elsewhere, suggesting the real strategy is to anticipate where status is heading rather than where it currently resides.

posts.summary: The post explores how single-LP venture capital models could drive more innovation in funding. Asparouhova highlights firms like Kapor Capital, Omidyar Network, Bloomberg Beta, and Breakout Labs as examples of single-funder structures that blend social impact with investment. She argues that when a single patient LP replaces traditional fund structures, investors gain flexibility to fund unconventional or longer-horizon projects. The core thesis is that tech wealth channeled through these models can revive the original spirit of philanthropy: giving society the freedom to take risks on things that aren't traditionally venture-backable.
key_points: Most VC reform discourse focuses on investment thesis or firm management, but changing who funds VCs is an overlooked lever for innovation
Single-LP funds like Kapor Capital and Omidyar Network blend philanthropic mission with venture investing, enabling social impact focus
Patient, single-LP structures free investors from traditional IRR pressure, allowing them to fund longer or less conventional paths to exit
Tech wealth has a unique opportunity to restore philanthropy's original purpose: funding risk-taking that produces public goods not suited to traditional venture backing
money_quotes: Another area I've been mulling over is changing who funds VCs, and whether single-LP models can lead to more innovation in the funding landscape.
What do the financial returns look like on models like these? I have no idea. And maybe that's the point.
Every traditional venture firm worries about delivering a healthy IRR to their LPs. With a single LP who's willing to be patient and experiment, investors can be more flexible with what they fund.
Tech wealth has an unprecedented chance to bring us back to the original intent of philanthropy: to provide society with the freedom to take risks.
Penicillin, the Red Cross, our 9-1-1 system, and many other of today's can't-live-withouts (that weren't venture-backable) began.
posts.key_insight: Single-LP venture models can restore philanthropy's original purpose by freeing investors from traditional return pressures, enabling them to fund the kind of risky, unconventional work that historically produced society's most important innovations.

posts.summary: Reflecting on Y Combinator's Female Founder Conference, Asparouhova argues that the event succeeded precisely because it avoided centering discussions on gender disadvantages or sexism. Instead, speakers shared substantive advice about building companies, allowing them to come across as multidimensional individuals rather than representatives of a demographic. She critiques the proliferation of identity-based sectors in entrepreneurship, arguing that siloing social impact into categories makes people one-dimensional. The post concludes that true inclusion comes from breaking down sectors rather than building new ones.
key_points: The Female Founder Conference succeeded by focusing on substance (how to build companies) rather than identity-based grievances
Building identity-based sectors (women entrepreneurship, social entrepreneurship, etc.) risks making people one-dimensional and siloing social impact
Exclusion is multidimensional—introversion, non-technical backgrounds, and office geography can matter as much as gender or race
True inclusion comes from breaking down categorical labels, not creating new ones
Meritocratic framing, often criticized, can paradoxically create more authentic and empowering spaces
money_quotes: We are awash in sectors that are ostensibly trying to make business better.
Y Combinator's conference demonstrated what happens when we focus not on building sectors, but rather breaking them down.
Not every female founder wants to be known as a female founder. Not every female VC wants to be known as a female VC.
Some of us just want to be really good at what we do, and recognized for that among our peers: male and female.
Building sectors is dangerous because it makes us one-dimensional.
A straight white male may still feel left out for not being technical, being the quiet one in a room, or being relegated to a corner of the office.
By not affixing the same, flat, 'women X' label onto everyone, the Female Founder Conference allowed everyone in attendance to breathe as themselves.
posts.key_insight: Identity-based sectors intended to promote inclusion can paradoxically flatten individuality, and the most empowering spaces are those that let people transcend their demographic labels.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on the role of creativity in shaping identity and building confidence. She distinguishes between creativity in professional work and creativity as a personal practice, valuing unstructured time that allows serendipitous ideas to emerge. She argues that creative pursuits outside of work help us probe the edges of our identities and clarify our values. Most importantly, she contends that regular creative expression builds the confidence to be vulnerable and authentic, which carries over into all areas of life.
key_points: Creativity in work means proactive problem solving rather than reactive execution
Personal creative time is less about specific activities and more about unstructured space for serendipitous thinking
Creative pursuits help shape identity by letting us experiment without consequences
Regular creative expression builds confidence to be vulnerable and authentic in public
When we believe our ideas are valuable, doing the right thing becomes easier
money_quotes: For me, it's less about the specific activity and more about having unstructured time to sit with my thoughts and see what bubbles up. Creativity is serendipity.
Creative time plays into the work that we do because it helps shape our identities.
It gives us a space to probe at the edges of who we are, try new things without worrying about the consequences, and in doing so, affirm or deny new aspects of our identities.

Putting something into the world "just because", whether that's a painting or a choreographed act or just wearing something different or doing something silly in public, gives us confidence that we can be our most vulnerable and authentic selves and survive the judgment of others.
It reaffirms that our ideas are valuable. And when we believe our ideas are valuable, that makes "doing the right thing", even when it's the hard thing, so much easier elsewhere in life.
posts.key_insight: Regular creative expression outside of work builds the confidence to be vulnerable and authentic, which in turn makes it easier to act on our convictions in all areas of life.

posts.summary: The post responds to a debate between Chris Dixon and Ev Williams about whether the internet is destined to become dominated by proprietary, centralized services over open protocols. Dixon warned we're on the wrong side of history, while Williams argued that media industries naturally settle into closed systems. Asparouhova offers several reasons for optimism: empowered content creators, low barriers to building products, the rise of patronage models, and blockchain technology. She argues the internet is fundamentally different from radio or television because it is not merely a distribution channel but an amalgamation of many channels. Her conclusion is that while individual channels may cycle through innovation and centralization, the internet's rapid pace means empires are built and broken overnight.
key_points: Content creators with enough critical mass can always take their audience elsewhere, creating tension with centralized platforms
The low barrier to building products gives more people agency over shaping the internet
Patronage models and blockchain technology could shift power away from centralized platforms
The internet differs fundamentally from radio or TV because it is an amalgamation of many distribution channels, not a single one
Individual channels may centralize, but the rapid cycle of innovation on the internet means the pendulum keeps swinging
money_quotes: In the end, with enough critical mass, mini moguls can always take their audience elsewhere.
The easier it is for anybody to shape the internet, the more agency people will want over their ideas.
Unlike radio or television, the internet is more than just media. The internet is how we work, think, and communicate.
Radio or television ARE distribution channels; the internet is an amalgamation of many of those channels - many radios or televisions - and new ones are being created all the time.
Maybe each channel is doomed to go through the cycle of innovation -> centralization, but those cycles repeat themselves rapidly on the internet.
Empires are built and broken overnight, and the pendulum will continue to swing back and forth.
posts.key_insight: The internet is not a single distribution channel like radio or TV but an amalgamation of many channels, so while individual platforms may centralize, the rapid creation of new channels ensures the pendulum between openness and centralization keeps swinging.

posts.summary: The post argues that what society labels as 'laziness' enabled by technology is actually cognitive progress. Asparouhova traces a pattern from calculators to GPS to on-demand services, showing that each generation fears new tools breed laziness, yet those tools eventually become accepted and unremarkable. She uses the programming world as her central analogy, where layers of abstraction have reduced menial coding while enabling more sophisticated thinking. Citing the Flynn effect—rising IQ scores driven by improved abstract reasoning—she concludes that technology isn't making us lazier but smarter by freeing us for higher cognitive work.
key_points: Every generation labels the next generation's tools as laziness-inducing, from calculators to GPS to concierge apps, yet each becomes normalized over time
Programming's evolution from reference books to libraries and frameworks demonstrates how automating menial work enables more sophisticated output
The Flynn effect shows IQ gains come specifically from abstract and hypothetical reasoning, not memorization—suggesting automation of rote tasks fuels cognitive advancement
Discomfort with on-demand services reflects the same unfounded anxiety that earlier generations had about calculators and GPS
money_quotes: Nobody thinks using a GPS or a calculator is a sign of laziness anymore. But there are other things that we think are making us lazy today.
Less time spent on menial coding means more time to think smartly about what you're building.
Less time spent on menial tasks means more time for humans to focus on higher cognitive goals.
New technologies aren't making us lazier. They're making us smarter.
Our increase in IQ comes not from improvement on concrete or memorization tasks, but on those requiring abstractions and hypotheticals.
Better analysis of hypothetical situations means more innovation.
posts.key_insight: What each generation dismisses as technology-enabled laziness is actually the automation of menial tasks that frees humans for higher-order thinking and innovation.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on her decision to leave Collaborative Fund, framing it through Milan Kundera's concept of 'Es muss sein' — the idea that our most meaningful choices feel less like free will and more like inescapable fate. She joined to work on an experimental growth fund but was redirected to deploying a seed stage fund. Despite loving the organization and its mission, she spent a year trying to suppress the pull toward something else. She announces her departure with gratitude and signals her next focus: finding new ways to direct capital toward valuable internet-powered ideas.
key_points: Our most meaningful life choices feel not like free will but like inescapable imperatives — fate rather than decision
Even when external circumstances seem ideal ('paper lives check all the boxes'), internal callings cannot be permanently suppressed
She valued Collaborative Fund's mission deeply but recognized that the specific role diverged from where she felt most compelled to work
Her next direction — directing capital toward valuable ideas powering the internet — foreshadows her later work on open source sustainability
money_quotes: We do not make them out of free will; our choices are an inescapable fate, an "imperative enslaving us".
For the past year I have tried to avoid my fate. But we can't ignore the things that scream most loudly in our heads, even when our paper lives seem to check all the boxes.
Venture capital showed me the weird and wonderful nature of the human condition.
It also gave me deep faith in the capacity of my fellow humans for kindness and generosity.
Finding new ways to direct capital towards valuable ideas that power the internet.
posts.key_insight: The most important life transitions come not from rational deliberation but from an inner imperative that becomes impossible to ignore, even when everything on paper looks right.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on her uncle FM-2030, a futurist and transhumanist who had his body cryonically suspended, and how his seemingly outlandish beliefs about the future now feel prescient. She argues that technological change is exponential rather than linear, citing Kurzweil, meaning the next 15 years will bring unimaginable transformation. This realization leads her to conclude that how we conduct ourselves daily matters enormously. Rather than focusing on grand technological projects, she asks whether our behavior makes society more thoughtful, collaborative, and humane.
key_points: Her uncle FM-2030 was ridiculed for his futurist beliefs, but exponential technological change has vindicated his sense of urgency about the near future
Technological progress is exponential, not linear, meaning past experience is a poor predictor of what comes next
The accelerating pace of change makes existential stakes feel immediate and real rather than science fiction
The most important response to this reality is not building grand technology but conducting oneself with thoughtfulness and humanity in daily life
money_quotes: Nobody could quantify when 'the future' would happen, but the stuff of sci fi novels was obviously light years away.
The older I get, the more I understand that thousands of years of past data cannot predict the future.
It is not farfetched to suggest that we could see the destruction of humankind within our lifetimes. That is not sci fi. That is real.
Suddenly, how we spend our days feels a lot more important.
Does your day-to-day behavior make our society more thoughtful and collaborative, sustain rather than destruct, unite rather than polarize?
Do you, as an individual, affirm our humanity?
posts.key_insight: In an era of exponential change where existential stakes feel immediate, the most meaningful response is not technological ambition but daily conduct that affirms our shared humanity.

posts.summary: The post examines why people don't share their authentic selves on social media, arguing that the problem is structural rather than cultural. Online platforms lack the nonverbal communication tools we rely on offline to give and receive empathy. Social media's limited response mechanisms—primarily the 'like' button—can only convey positive reactions, making it uncomfortable for users to engage with sad or vulnerable content. This creates a feedback loop where posters who share difficult emotions receive silence, discouraging future authenticity. The author points to Buzzfeed's nuanced reaction options as a step in the right direction.
key_points: Sharing online is fundamentally an act of seeking validation, and social scientist John Gottman's research on 'bids' shows that unreciprocated emotional bids damage relationships
Online communication is constrained to verbal cues, and we've developed workarounds like emojis, letter-stretching, and GIFs as substitutes for nonverbal expression
The 'like' button only conveys positive responses, leaving users unable to express sympathy, sadness, or nuanced emotional reactions
The absence of appropriate response mechanisms creates a chilling effect: posters who share vulnerable content receive silence, which discourages authentic sharing
A broader range of reaction options beyond binary like/dislike could meaningfully change the type of content people feel comfortable sharing
money_quotes: As it stands today, I don't think we can expect people to share their unfiltered selves in a one-to-many format.
When we share something about ourselves, online or offline, we're looking for validation from our listeners.
If somebody shares that they are feeling sad on Facebook, it makes people uncomfortable because they are unable to exhibit an appropriate emotional response.
A 'like' is meant to be the equivalent of a smile or encouraging nod, but there is no way for us to frown or give a sympathetic nod.
This is as frustrating as though in the physical world, we were unable to do anything but smile.
Most readers simply won't say anything at all, leaving the poster feeling that nobody is listening to them, and making them less likely to post this type of story in the future.
posts.key_insight: Social media's limited emotional vocabulary—particularly the binary like button—structurally prevents authentic self-expression by making it impossible to respond with empathy to anything other than positive content.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on a period of quiet exploration and iteration after stepping back from public writing. She describes the discomfort of uncertainty and not having a clear narrative for what she's working on. A friend's remark about persisting because one still has 'something interesting left to say' resonates deeply. She rejects the 'entrepreneur' label in favor of thinking of herself as an artist, committed to continuing her work regardless of timeline or outcome.
key_points: Periods of silence can be productive: listening, testing theories, and revising beliefs happen offline
The discomfort of not having a neat answer to 'what are you up to?' reflects society's demand for legible career narratives
Persistence in creative work comes from an internal sense of having something left to say, not external validation
The artist identity, unlike the entrepreneur identity, permits open-ended timelines and uncertain outcomes
money_quotes: Some of the things I felt most sure about, I've now rejected.
I keep doing what I do, even when everybody around me doesn't get it, because I feel like I still have something interesting left to say.
I'm not an entrepreneur. I never liked the label for myself. I'd rather think of myself as an artist.
Sometimes it's not clear what exactly needs to be said, or what form it will take, or even whether it will have any impact on the world.
Not knowing the future is scary sometimes. It's extremely uncomfortable to be in a period of iterating, of spending some days so elated and other days so low, sometimes within 24 hours of each other.
posts.key_insight: The drive to keep creating comes not from knowing the outcome, but from the persistent feeling that you still have something interesting left to say.

posts.summary: The post examines the limitations of crowdfunding as a funding model, sparked by debate over Marco Arment's decision to fund his podcast app Overcast 2 through patronage. Asparouhova argues that crowdfunding is essentially a marketing campaign, which disadvantages skilled creators who lack strong personal brands. She frames crowdfunding as an evolutionary step in financing—just as AngelList syndicates evolved venture capital and Kickstarter evolved friends-and-family rounds—and asks what the next evolution beyond crowdfunding might look like. The core tension she identifies is that society has validated a desire to fund creativity, but the current mechanism rewards self-promotion over creative merit. She points to AngelList Syndicates as a model that aggregates crowd capital while simplifying the experience for recipients, and asks what an equivalent would look like for creative crowdfunding.
key_points: Crowdfunding is fundamentally a marketing campaign, requiring creators to solicit many small checks rather than one large one, which favors those with strong personal brands
The debate around Overcast 2's patronage model reveals an uncomfortable tension: patronage works for people with established brands but leaves most creators behind
Crowdfunding validates society's desire to see creativity exist in the world, but the marketing-heavy nature of platforms like Kickstarter is a bug, not a feature
Many of the most interesting creators are poorly disposed to personal brand building, because the most interesting ideas are often the most unpopular
AngelList Syndicates offer a model for crowdfunding's next evolution: aggregating crowd capital while presenting a single check to the recipient
money_quotes: One of the biggest limitations of crowdfunding is that it is essentially a marketing campaign.
If we acknowledge that creative contributions are valuable to society, the marketing-heavy nature of running a successful Kickstarter campaign starts to look more like a bug than a feature.
Surely we can agree that not every skilled creator has a strong personal brand, nor cares to build one.
Some of the most interesting ideas are also the most unpopular.
Crowdfunding validates our desire to see creativity exist in the world.
AngelList syndicates are the next evolution of venture capital. Kickstarter is the next evolution of friends-and-family rounds (or having a rich uncle).
posts.key_insight: Crowdfunding has validated society's desire to fund creativity, but its reliance on personal brand and marketing means the next evolution must find ways to aggregate crowd capital without requiring creators to be self-promoters.

posts.summary: Nadia responds to Ian Bogost's Atlantic article arguing programmers shouldn't call themselves engineers. She concedes that many modern developers assemble prefabricated code rather than building infrastructure, but argues that a class of true software engineers does exist: those who build and maintain the digital infrastructure everyone else depends on. Using OpenSSL and the Heartbleed bug as her central example, she shows that a tiny team of volunteers maintained code used by two-thirds of the world's web servers. She draws a direct parallel between the pride and responsibility of civil engineers and open source maintainers, arguing that dismissing all software workers ignores those toiling thanklessly beneath the surface.
key_points: Modern software development often involves assembling prefabricated components rather than deep engineering, which partially validates Bogost's claim
Digital infrastructure—developer tools, languages, libraries, frameworks—is as real and consequential as physical infrastructure like bridges
OpenSSL, used by two-thirds of the world's web servers, was maintained by 11 volunteers with less than $2,000/year in donations before Heartbleed
The media stereotype of software developers as overpaid and self-entitled erases the invisible maintainers who make modern programming possible
True software engineers exist precisely because they've made their work invisible—making everyone else's jobs look easy
money_quotes: We have digital infrastructure, too.
Digital infrastructure may not be as permanent as physical infrastructure. It may change frequently. But millions of people still depend on digital infrastructure to function.
These tools are actively maintained by volunteers who work relentlessly, without pay, to ensure a programmer's favorite Python http library doesn't break.
Perhaps, if these software engineers make a mistake, people will not die, as with the civil engineers building bridges. But bad things can still happen.
It's become acceptable in tech media to portray software developers as self-entitled, overpaid neckbeards.
To perpetuate these stereotypes ignores those toiling thanklessly under the hood to give those overpaid 'programmers' and 'developers' such a cushy job in the first place.

If Bogost is correct, and today's computer programmers don't really do that much complicated work, then we must acknowledge there is still a quiet class of true 'software engineers' who are working hard to make it easy on the rest of us.
They're making it so easy for us, in fact, that we forget they're even there.
posts.key_insight: The very invisibility of open source infrastructure maintainers—true software engineers—is proof of their success, not evidence of their nonexistence.

posts.summary: The post argues that Zuckerberg's decision to use an LLC rather than a nonprofit for the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative reflects a broader shift in how Silicon Valley approaches philanthropy, not a rejection of it. Today's philanthropists want measurable returns on social investment, accountability, and flexible vehicles rather than traditional charitable giving. The author traces this from the 'mea culpa' era of 19th-20th century philanthropy through the Gates era to a new model where VCs function as modern foundations and startups serve social missions. She notes that this shift was already advocated by philanthropy reformers pushing for business concepts in the nonprofit world. The LLC structure simply provides more flexibility to fund grants, loans, and market-based solutions. Rather than criticizing the structure, the author argues we should hold the initiative accountable to its stated mission.
key_points: Modern philanthropists want investment-style returns on social spending, favoring models like Kiva and Kickstarter that invest in beneficiaries rather than traditional charity
An LLC structure is arguably more effective than a nonprofit for flexible philanthropic deployment across grants, loans, and venture-style bets
VCs are becoming the new foundations, funding infrastructure-type investments and mission-driven startups in health, food, and urban areas
Silicon Valley's philanthropy mirrors its political views: government and social services should enable individuals rather than protect them from capitalism
The Chan Zuckerberg Initiative actually advances ideas that philanthropy reformers like Dan Pallotta and the impact investing movement have long advocated
money_quotes: This generation of philanthropists, big or small, wants to see a return on their social investment.
They despise financial leeches, charities begging for money in, money out, and are excited by models that help other people help themselves.
If philanthropy is an investment portfolio, an LLC allows dollars to go towards grants, loans, crazy big bets, and everything in between.
In this new era of philanthropy, one could argue that VCs are the new foundations.
From any one of these companies flows a thousand creations.
Let's call the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative what it is: a radical, albeit expensive, experiment in philanthropy.
The Chan Zuckerberg Initiative, LLC, is more a symptom of our zeitgeist than a snubbing of philanthropy.
posts.key_insight: The Chan Zuckerberg LLC isn't a rejection of philanthropy but a symptom of Silicon Valley's investment-oriented zeitgeist, where flexible structures enabling measurable social returns are replacing traditional charitable giving.

posts.summary: Asparouhova examines the paradox that 40% of millennials support government censorship of speech offensive to minorities, despite being the most tolerant generation on social issues. She argues this isn't really about government policy but about a new form of peer-enforced censorship enabled by social media, where outrage content spreads fastest because it reinforces identity. Drawing on Scott Alexander's 'toxoplasma of rage' concept, she suggests that online mob dynamics and public shaming function as de facto censorship even without government action. She concludes that while the impulse comes from good intentions, society must decide whether it still values free speech as a civil right.
key_points: 40% of millennials support government censorship of speech offensive to minorities, contradicting both First Amendment principles and their own reputation as the most tolerant generation
Increased access to information has paradoxically made people more inclined toward censorship rather than less, which is counterintuitive
Online outrage culture functions as a form of distributed censorship through public shaming and mob dynamics, regardless of government involvement
The spread of extreme perspectives online is driven by identity reinforcement rather than genuine ideological shifts in the population
The protection of free speech matters most in edge cases involving speech people find offensive, not speech everyone agrees with
money_quotes: It seems counterintuitive that increased access to information online should make us want to censor more, not less.
How does one mesh that utopian worldview with the desire to censor others who are different?
These are the edge cases where the protection of free speech matters most.
The content that pisses us off the most is also the content we share the most, because it strengthens our identity.
Someone wrote an offensive tweet or made an off-color remark? May a thousand fires rain upon them! These days, with enough angry retweets, anyone can censor anything.
Writing an angry post about so-and-so's latest offensive remark is technically an exercise of your right to free speech, but as part of a critical mass, also contributes to censorship of your peers.

World peace is here, but it's a little dystopian. Rather than the colorful patchwork quilt envisioned by 1960s free-love hippies, we've got citizens policing adherence to a politically correct set of views.
posts.key_insight: Modern censorship is no longer a government undertaking but a distributed social phenomenon where online outrage mobs enforce conformity through public shaming, making each individual's angry retweet a small act of collective censorship.

posts.summary: The post examines the apparent contradiction in Silicon Valley's reactions to two concurrent issues: Apple's refusal to build an FBI backdoor and India's ban on Facebook's Free Basics. Asparouhova argues that people who support one but oppose the other reveal an inconsistent trust framework—trusting either government or corporations but not applying equal scrutiny to both. She contends that both governments and companies are run by fallible humans and deserve the same checks and balances. The core warning is that the tech world risks replacing one benevolent dictator (government) with another (corporations) if it doesn't demand accountability from both.
key_points: The Apple-FBI and Free Basics controversies reveal inconsistent attitudes toward power: people tend to trust either government or corporations but rarely scrutinize both equally.
Supporting Free Basics while opposing backdoors amounts to replacing faith in government with uncritical faith in tech companies—swapping one benevolent dictator for another.
Governments were never truly objective or neutral, as historical examples like the Three-Fifths Compromise demonstrate.
All power is subject to human error regardless of whether it resides in the public or private sector.
As technology reshapes governance, the goal should be creating checks and balances that apply to all concentrations of power.
money_quotes: But humans are humans, whether they work for the government or for a large tech company.
The private and public sector deserve equal scrutiny.
I believe all power is subject to human error.
To support Free Basics but oppose backdoors would be to commit the same fallacy as our predecessors. We are still putting faith in a benevolent dictator (companies) instead of in ourselves.
As we reimagine government in a world driven by technology, let's not accidentally replace one benevolent dictator with another.
Our goal should be to create a system of checks and balances that apply to everyone.
The government was never objective or neutral.
posts.key_insight: Tech culture risks repeating history's mistakes by transferring uncritical trust from government to corporations, when both deserve equal scrutiny and checks on their power.

posts.summary: Asparouhova responds to the expulsion of Larry Garfield from the Drupal community over his private BDSM lifestyle, arguing that the justification has merely shifted from conservative moral grounds to progressive ones while producing the same unjust outcome. She notes that Drupal founder Dries Buytaert framed the decision not as opposition to BDSM itself but as opposition to a philosophy seen as threatening gender equality. Asparouhova contends that persecuting people for their private beliefs, regardless of the ideological framework used, is fundamentally incompatible with a free society. She implicates herself and her readers in creating a political climate where coercion masquerades as equality.
key_points: The justification for ostracizing BDSM practitioners has shifted from 'threat to family values' to 'threat to gender equality,' but the result is identical persecution
Beliefs are not actions, and punishing people for private beliefs undermines free and open democracy regardless of the moral framework invoked
The broader community bears collective responsibility for a climate where coercion masquerades as equality and nuance is dismissed as a moral question
Diversity as an enforced orthodoxy risks becoming its own form of conformity
money_quotes: In the past, Dries might've kicked Larry out because 'BDSM is a threat to family values'. Today, leaders like Dries kick Larry out because 'BDSM is a threat to gender equality'. Unfortunately, the end result is the same.
Beliefs are not actions. We cannot persecute people for what they believe, no matter how much it disgusts us, and simultaneously maintain a free and open democracy.
We are all responsible for the increasingly alarming practice of coercion masquerading as equality.
If you're upset about Dries's decision (and it seems that many are), just remember: you created this.
Diversity is starting to look a lot more like conformity.
If diversity is our dogma, call me 'spiritual, not religious'. I still pray for the same things as you, but I won't be at the witch trials.
posts.key_insight: When ideological orthodoxy—whether conservative or progressive—is used to punish private beliefs rather than harmful actions, diversity becomes its own form of conformity.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on her complex psychological response to sexual misconduct and abuse of power in the tech industry and beyond. She describes how she long prided herself on staying silent about her own experiences, only to realize that silence was a form of self-protection that also protected her abusers. Reading Ronan Farrow's nuanced New Yorker piece about Weinstein broke something open for her, because unlike reductive media portrayals, it captured the messy reality of how victims normalize trauma. She explores why binary media narratives of good vs. evil feel alienating to actual victims, who experience self-doubt, continued relationships with abusers, and internalized shame. Ultimately, she concludes that true power will come not from public confession or silence, but from genuine indifference toward the people who harmed her.
key_points: Victims of misconduct often normalize their experiences through silence, self-blame, and maintaining relationships with abusers, which is a predictable trauma response rather than evidence that nothing happened
Binary media portrayals of sexual misconduct as clear-cut good vs. evil alienate actual victims, whose experiences are tangled with ambiguity, guilt, and self-doubt
Silence is not neutral — it actively bonds victims to their abusers by requiring constant effort to protect secrets and manage encounters
Women often report misconduct as a group not just for stronger evidence, but because collective action shifts the spotlight away from the victim's identity onto the perpetrator's
The essay reframes the conversation from being about specific incidents to the universal psychology of how people on the receiving end of power abuse cope and rationalize
money_quotes: Yes, of course, we all go through these things. Some of us just don't talk about it.
The binary media portrayal is written by people looking for a story, one that requires flattening its characters into a paper battle of good vs. evil.
The declarations of 'we have to do better' (sexual misconduct's version of 'thoughts and prayers').
The transgressions that tend to be widely reported are often, paradoxically, the least vicious.
I've spent my whole life trying to normalize trauma. And part of that process meant staying silent.
Being this quiet, and working this hard to make sure the stories don't get out, means I am only bonding myself even more irrevocably to these people. Love and hate are equal and opposing forces.
When it's a group of women against one person, the spotlight shines on that person alone. Victims don't have to be protagonists in a class-action story, and that's a relief.
This isn't a story about sex, assault, or even harassment. It's the story that accompanies every abuse of power.
posts.key_insight: Silence around abuse of power is not passive indifference but an active, exhausting process of self-protection that paradoxically binds victims more tightly to their abusers.

posts.summary: Asparouhova critiques voting as a governance mechanism, arguing it was designed for 18th-century conditions that no longer apply. She identifies three core flaws: voting can never be truly representative (especially in decentralized communities where the total population is unknown), it promotes zero-sum competition rather than cooperation, and it creates a veneer of objectivity that absolves people of the hard work of compromise. Drawing on cooperative board games and modern open source governance models like lazy consensus, she proposes cooperative mechanics as an alternative framework. She suggests systems with enforced deadlines, auto-renewals, expiration dates, and fail-deadlies that incentivize action and collaboration rather than opposition. The post frames our current era as a 'stag hunt' where cooperative governance is both possible and necessary.
key_points: Voting was designed for small, homogeneous populations in the 1700s and doesn't scale to modern decentralized communities where the total population is unknown
Voting promotes zero-sum competition and faction formation rather than cooperation, and the U.S. founders failed to anticipate political parties spanning across branches of government
On-chain governance and over-engineered systems create a 'veneer of objectivity' that lets people avoid the hard work of disagreement and compromise

Cooperative governance models like lazy consensus (used in Node.js and Rust communities) flip incentives so objecting is expensive and inaction is the default, rewarding reputation over mere participation
Reputation is becoming more powerful than money as a social currency, and cooperative systems can leverage this more democratically than voting systems
money_quotes: If we're coming up with wild ideas for social coordination, voting seems like an awfully familiar solution.

Over-engineered systems create a 'veneer of objectivity' that lets us put blind faith into something greater than ourselves — and in doing so, absolve ourselves of the very real, hard work of disagreeing and compromising with our fellow humans.
If we ever get to the point where we blindly accept what a bunch of code tells us to do, machines will have won and humanity, in all its creative chaos, will sink into decline.
Under voting, it's cheap to veto (i.e. vote against something), which has led to an anti-pattern of outrage today. Not only is it cheap to disagree, but you're often rewarded for it.
Cooperative mechanics flip this around, so it's cheap to do nothing and expensive to raise an objection. That means people reserve their objections for stuff they really care about.
It's impossible to maintain deep knowledge of every political topic of importance. Cooperative governance gives us permission to be curators, focusing on just our areas of passion and expertise.
Votes can be manipulated by propaganda and special interests. Reputation takes time to build, but it belongs to you.
Voting wasn't designed for scale, and it's not going to carry us through the next era of governance.
posts.key_insight: Voting is an 18th-century solution poorly suited to modern decentralized governance; cooperative mechanics that make objection expensive and default to action offer a more scalable alternative.

posts.summary: The post examines why software developers struggle to monetize their own code despite their skills being in high demand. It traces three historical phases of developer income: selling code (1980s-90s), selling time/skills (late 1990s-present), and an emerging model of selling access based on reputation. Code is hard to monetize because it has zero marginal cost and developers can easily build alternatives to existing tools. Employment solves the income problem but forces developers to write code for others rather than themselves. The post argues that the future of developer economics lies in monetizing reputation and identity rather than code or time.
key_points: Developers' code-writing skills have market value, but the code they independently produce often has none, creating a fundamental tension
Code is difficult to monetize for two reasons: zero marginal cost enabling free sharing, and developers' ability and desire to build their own alternatives
Selling time through employment or freelancing is a workaround that funds developers' lifestyles but doesn't let them profit from their own public code
As automation advances, the amount of code that needs to be written by humans is shrinking, threatening the 'sell time' model long-term
Reputation-based income — charging for prioritization, knowledge, or brand — may be the emerging model because a developer's reputation has fewer substitutes than their code
money_quotes: While their code-writing skills have market value, their output (the code they write) has none.
Selling to developers a bit like selling ice to an Eskimo.
Information wants to be freely shared, despite our best efforts to make it behave otherwise.
Over time, we don't need to write as much code as we used to.
Selling time doesn't allow a developer to make money producing their own code. It merely funds their lifestyle so they can write other code in their spare time.
Maybe it's not the projects, but the people behind the projects, that carry the most value.
