So long as we focus on code, we'll keep coming back to the problem of substitutes. Code has many substitutes. Getting paid to write code has many substitutes. But a developer's reputation has fewer substitutes.
In the early 1990s, it seemed inconceivable that people wouldn't pay for software. Today, it might seem inconceivable that developers might eventually not sell their time.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental challenge for developers is that code and coding skills are endlessly substitutable, so lasting value lies not in what developers produce but in the irreplaceable reputations they build.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova argues that online outrage is largely a byproduct of platform lock-in, applying Albert Hirschman's exit/voice framework to social media. When users cannot meaningfully leave platforms due to network effects and data ownership, they resort to 'voice' — which manifests as outrage. She explores two forms of exit: off-platform migration (hampered by data lock-in and network effects) and on-platform exits like private messaging, blocking tools, and groups that let users create smaller communities. She suggests that rather than building new platforms or doubling down on regulation, the better path may be enabling users to 'shard' into smaller, context-rich communities within existing platforms, even if this risks creating knowledge ghettos where influencers retreat from public discourse.
key_points: Online outrage can be understood as dissent without an outlet — when users can't exit platforms, they resort to voice, which manifests as anger and conflict
Regulation paradoxically strengthens platform lock-in by investing in 'voice' rather than enabling meaningful alternatives
Platforms hold legal sovereignty but natural sovereignty lies in the network of users themselves; platforms have no inherent value beyond being agreed-upon gathering points
On-platform exits — private messaging, muting, groups, stories — may be more viable than off-platform migration for diffusing outrage
Federated communities risk creating 'knowledge ghettos' where influencers retreat to private spaces, leaving public discourse impoverished like underfunded public schools
money_quotes: Maybe outrage is dissent without an outlet, a byproduct of platform lock-in with nowhere to hide and nowhere else to go.
When people can't exit, they resort to voice, which looks a lot like outrage.
By investing in voice, we're doubling down on a bad relationship instead of exploring alternatives.
Twitter has no inherent value, except that it's where we agree to gather, find, and talk to each other.
Facebook isn't a community. It's a landowner: the wide plains and open skies to which communities flock.
If platforms treat 'exit' as a feature rather than a bug, they can retain their users by offering spaces for smaller, context-rich interactions.
If social media is a public space, history tells us that public spaces tend to become under-resourced, partly because the elite can afford better options.
To do nothing is autocracy.
posts.key_insight: Online outrage is best understood not as a cultural failing but as the predictable consequence of platform lock-in — when users cannot exit, their only recourse is voice, and the most promising solution is enabling smaller, self-organized communities within existing platforms.

posts.summary: Nadia announces she is leaving GitHub to join Protocol Labs, where she will research and develop new economic models for software production. She frames this move within the broader challenge of understanding how digital infrastructure gets made and how incentives work in the modern tech ecosystem. She will also serve as a visiting Fellow at Harvard Kennedy School. She reflects on her time at GitHub and its responsibility to the open source world, and expresses excitement about Protocol Labs' alignment with long-timeline, hard-problem research.
key_points: Digital infrastructure production is poorly understood, and current research lacks frameworks for today's incentive challenges
Open source software's thorough documentation makes it an ideal lens for studying behavioral and economic patterns in digital production
Paid research opportunities at the intersection of tech and social science are extremely rare, making the Protocol Labs role uniquely valuable
Protocol Labs' organizational structure and funding model are designed for long-timeline, hard-problem research
GitHub's challenge lies in balancing the needs of enormously diverse developer communities and project types
money_quotes: We know shockingly little about how to structure incentives in our modern world, and current research doesn't have much to say about today's challenges.
Open source software, because it's so thoroughly documented, is a great way to understand these behavioral patterns.
It turns out that thinking about these problems on my nights and weekends is a recipe for creative exhaustion.
There are few companies who not only share my interest in solving hard problems with long timelines, but who structure their organization and funding model accordingly.
GitHub has a huge responsibility to the world of open source software, and I feel lucky to have taken part in carrying that weight, if only for a brief time.
posts.key_insight: Understanding how digital infrastructure gets produced requires dedicated, funded research into economic incentives — something the tech world has largely neglected despite its importance.

posts.summary: Nadia reviews Elinor Ostrom's 'Governing the Commons,' which challenges the assumption that common resources inevitably suffer from tragedies like free-riding and the prisoner's dilemma. Ostrom demonstrated through case studies that communities can sustainably manage shared resources without market or state intervention, given conditions like defined boundaries, localization, and mutual accountability. Nadia connects this directly to open source infrastructure, arguing that sustainable management requires high context among participants and that context collapse explains most failure modes. She suggests that rather than seeking a single fix for open source sustainability, we should catalog behavioral differences across communities and trust developers to self-organize. The essay concludes by speculating that Ostrom's ideas may finally find their moment in an era of institutional distrust and decentralization.
key_points: Ostrom's work adds a third possible outcome to commons management beyond market or state intervention: successful community self-governance under the right conditions
Sustainably managing the commons requires high mutual context among participants, and most failures trace back to context collapse
Key conditions include defined community boundaries, localized rules, and monitoring/enforcement mechanisms
Open source sustainability is better served by high-context self-organized solutions (like funding key contributors) than low-context ones (like bounties for strangers)
Ostrom's framework resists universal models, insisting every situation is different and localized—which itself is a central insight
money_quotes: Sustainably managing the commons requires a high degree of context among participants. Most failure outcomes can be traced back to context collapse.
Managing at scale doesn't mean stuffing more and more people into the same community, but acknowledging boundaries and working together to govern at multiple levels.
Nobody wants to be told the answer is complicated, whereas the other models are simple, even if they're pessimistic and reductive.
Rather than trying to 'fix' it, perhaps the goal is to understand and catalog these behavioral differences, so that commonalities emerge between them.
Raising money to pay for a major contributor's work is a high-context, self-organized solution. Paying out bounties to strangers is a low-context solution.

Maybe now is the time to cast aside the market and the state as our saviors, and instead consider an alternative: one that recognizes the ability of people to organize and design solutions for themselves.
The underlying assumption is that without external intervention, individuals will act selfishly, without regard for collective interests.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable commons management—including open source—depends not on external intervention from markets or governments, but on high-context community self-governance with defined boundaries and mutual trust.

posts.summary: Asparouhova draws parallels between app developers and open source developers, arguing both are 'toolmakers' facing similar challenges: low revenue despite high adoption, entitled users, and platform dependence. Inspired by the documentary 'App: The Human Story' and The Developers Union, she compares Apple's App Store relationship with app developers to GitHub's relationship with open source developers. Both groups have resorted to open letters to platforms that don't maintain deep relationships with their power users. She suggests that platforms may be inherently misaligned with developer interests, and that regulation or revolution in distribution models may be more realistic than hoping for platform reform.
key_points: App developers and open source developers face strikingly similar challenges: low revenue despite high adoption, entitled users demanding free work, and dependence on platforms that don't adequately represent their interests.

Platforms like Apple's App Store and GitHub may function more as aggregators capturing value for themselves rather than true platforms acting in developers' best interests, making reform unlikely without regulation or alternative distribution models.
Poor pricing models created by platform constraints lead users to undervalue developer work, fueling the entitlement both groups experience.
Successful open source developers get acquihired and go quiet, while successful app developers remain independent business owners with stronger incentives to advocate for systemic change.
Both groups are fundamentally 'toolmakers' — people who make software enabling others to do more — and might benefit from collaboration despite their cultural differences.
money_quotes: These platforms don't have existing deep relationships with their power users, nor have they taken time to understand them.
The ability to experiment with funding models is constrained by GitHub's policies and product, much like a tenant is constrained by their ability to modify an apartment that they've rented.
Open source projects on GitHub represent billions of dollars in value creation, but none of that is captured on GitHub.
Perhaps it's naive to assume that platforms will ever act in their users' best interests.
Those hoping for reform might be better served by regulation or revolution to align developer and consumer incentives.

Successful open source developers get 'acquihired' by a company who values their reputation and skills, which has the perverse effect of making them less noisy about how to make a living off of open source.

We're still a delightfully primitive society, connected at internet scale: someone turns a rock into a hammer, then hands it to a friend. Someone writes a better library, then publishes it for anyone to use.
Toolmakers play a critical role in accelerating human progress, and their work ought to be recognized and celebrated.
posts.key_insight: App developers and open source developers are both toolmakers trapped in platform-dependent ecosystems that structurally undervalue their work, and meaningful change likely requires regulation or new distribution models rather than hoping platforms will voluntarily reform.

posts.summary: Nadia explores the historical tradition of 'gentleman scientists' who funded their own research independently, arguing that academia need not be the gatekeeper of knowledge production. She highlights how independent researchers from Darwin to Norman Borlaug made breakthroughs precisely because they operated outside institutional constraints. While acknowledging the real challenge of lacking external validation, she argues that independent research doesn't have to be expensive and can be enabled by creative funding arrangements. Drawing on her own experience funding open source research through grants and later joining organizations like Protocol Labs, she advocates for more people to pursue independent inquiry as a form of public service.
key_points: Independent, self-funded research was common in the 18th-19th centuries and produced major breakthroughs, yet today we assume research requires institutional permission
The biggest challenge of independent research is not funding but validation — without institutional affiliation, researchers struggle to be taken seriously
Lack of formal training can paradoxically produce better results, as Norman Borlaug's 'mistake' of shuttle breeding accidentally created superior wheat varieties
Independent research doesn't have to be expensive, and funding can be cobbled together from grants, organizations, and creative arrangements
Publishing findings publicly regardless of outcomes is itself a worthy contribution to society and a viable alternative to ivory tower academia
money_quotes: It's sort of odd that we assume you need someone's permission to do research.
There's no reason that universities need to be the gatekeepers of exploring and developing new ideas.
It's better to say you're funded by a big institutional grant than to admit it's coming out of your savings account.
For the truly obsessed person, the need for validation isn't about ego; it's about sanity.
You want to know there's some meaning behind the dizzying mental labyrinth that you simultaneously can't escape and also never want to leave.
Life is short. Do whatever you can't stop thinking about.
Documenting your findings in public (regardless of outcomes!) is a worthy contribution to society, full stop.
You don't need a PhD to study something you care about. You don't need to publish papers in academic journals to become widely respected.
posts.key_insight: Independent research outside academia is a viable and historically proven path that produces breakthroughs precisely because it offers intellectual freedom unconstrained by institutional gatekeeping.

posts.summary: The post explores why cryptocurrency projects are so preoccupied with the threat of forks compared to open source software at large. The common explanation is that financial incentives in crypto make forking more attractive, but Nadia argues the real reason is simply that crypto is an early-stage ecosystem. She draws on Andrey Petrov's insight that momentum and community mass are the real defensibility in crypto, making forks difficult to sustain regardless of financial incentive. By comparing early open source culture (which also obsessed over forks) with contemporary open source (which largely doesn't), she concludes that crypto's fork anxiety is a symptom of ecosystem immaturity, not a unique property of financial incentives. She predicts crypto will eventually consolidate into fewer stable projects, mirroring what happened in open source and the broader tech startup ecosystem.
key_points: Financial incentives in crypto don't fundamentally change the dynamics of forking; momentum and community mass are the real defensibility against forks
Early open source communities were similarly preoccupied with forks, but as projects matured and ecosystems stabilized, forks became less of a practical concern
Forking a cryptocurrency means forking not just code but the ledger, community, miners, and third-party support — making successful forks extremely difficult
Many behaviors attributed as unique to crypto are actually characteristic of any early-stage open source ecosystem
Technology ecosystems tend to consolidate over time, and decentralized networks may follow the same pattern since networks benefit from monopolies
money_quotes: I think crypto is concerned about forks, not because financial incentives actually change the game, but simply because it's early days.
A fork basically starts from a stationary position and it takes herculean effort to reach any kind of relevant momentum.
Being committed to a large codebase is like mass, it takes proportional effort to ramp up or shift directions.
Realistically, from an open source perspective, anyone who has the motivation to start a genuine competing fork is signing up for a lifetime of thankless maintenance work.
The right to fork is still critical as an implied threat to keep the system in check. But major political forks aren't as common, and certainly not as top-of-mind, anymore.
I'm guessing there are other behaviors that we attribute to being unusually characteristic of crypto, which are actually just signs of an early-stage open source ecosystem.
Networks benefit from monopolies.
posts.key_insight: Crypto's preoccupation with forks is not caused by financial incentives but is a hallmark of any early-stage open source ecosystem, and will likely fade as the ecosystem matures and consolidates.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on the tension between privacy and impact in her Helium Grant program, which gives $1,000 grants to people pursuing interesting ideas. She discovered that both grantees and sponsors often prefer anonymity about their financial situations, yet growing the program requires public visibility. She draws parallels to broader internet culture, where achieving impact increasingly demands sacrificing privacy and authenticity. She concludes by defaulting to confidentiality, arguing that privacy is underrated and some good work is better done quietly.
key_points: Financial situations are sensitive in both directions: grant recipients may not want to broadcast needing help, and donors may not want to broadcast their giving
Growing impact requires visibility, creating an inherent conflict between privacy and scale for philanthropic efforts
Supporting only people you personally know is inefficient and borders on nepotism, but reaching strangers requires sacrificing privacy
Internet culture's demand for transparency and self-promotion can infect genuinely generous actions with a dystopian quality
Democratization of power on the internet seems to come at the cost of authenticity, as everyone must 'sing for their supper'
money_quotes: It means it's not necessarily obvious, after all, that everybody wants to broadcast their financial situation to the world.
In order to achieve more impact, I need to be less discreet.
Technology enables you to do pretty much anything you dream of, but only if enough people pay attention.
In the long run, democratization of power seems to come at the cost of authenticity.
Sometimes it's exhausting to stand on a podium with a megaphone, shouting about things that you wouldn't otherwise shout about, because everyone has to sing for their supper.
It's always struck me as funny that online transparency and online privacy attract similar activists, because to me, these things are often horrifyingly at odds with one another.
Doing nice things loudly, even when they seem obviously generous, can infect those actions with a bizarre, dystopian quality.
Privacy is underrated. Sometimes, it's nice to do things quietly on the internet.
posts.key_insight: The internet's demand for visibility creates a fundamental tension with privacy, making it difficult to do good at scale without forcing people to broadcast their financial situations and sacrifice authenticity.

posts.summary: The post surveys academic approaches to measuring open source project health, moving from simple contributor counts to more nuanced models. It begins with the 'fountain' model of contributors flowing in and out, then examines magnet-sticky frameworks that differentiate attraction from retention. The author argues that contributors are not fungible and that most research over-indexes on contributor counts, which fails for the many critical projects maintained by just one or two people. She advocates instead for activity-based metrics like merged pull requests (GPPR), drawing an analogy to GDP for nations. The post concludes that 'work done' is a more universal and resilient health metric than contributor counts, while acknowledging edge cases like stable projects with low activity.
key_points: Most existing research measures project health by tracking contributors entering and leaving, treating them as interchangeable, which ignores that maintainers are 'keystone species' whose departure has disproportionate impact.
The magnet-sticky framework offers a richer model by distinguishing a project's ability to attract new contributors from its ability to retain existing ones, yielding four health states.
Contributors are not fungible: at least half of contributors in popular projects are casual one-time participants accounting for less than 2% of commits, while maintainers produce the bulk of work.
Activity-based metrics like Gross Product Pull Requests (GPPR) better capture project health across projects of all sizes, including the many critical libraries with only one or two maintainers.
Stable, widely-used projects with low activity shouldn't be penalized; incorporating issue tracking alongside PRs can distinguish 'stable' from 'stagnant'.
money_quotes: My view is that we focus too much on contributors, rather than project activity, and that contributors are an inadequate health measure for the increasing number of smaller, but critical, open source projects today.
Contributors are not fungible.
Project maintainers are like a keystone species. Although there are fewer of them, removing a member of a keystone species has a disproportionately negative effect on the ecosystem.
Total number of contributors does not correlate to whether a project survives.
It doesn't matter how many people contribute or how many hours were put into it, so long as pull requests are being opened and merged.
Not every project will ever look like Django.

A project that's active, but declining in popularity, might indicate a sign of 'healthy decline', much like an old-growth forest, where users are migrating to another project that better suits their needs.
Maybe 'issue zero' and 'pull request zero', with a steady rate of issues/PRs being opened and closed, is the picture of project health.
posts.key_insight: Open source project health is better measured by work output like merged pull requests than by contributor counts, because contributors are not fungible and many critical projects thrive with very few maintainers.

posts.summary: Nadia analyzes three cryptocurrency open source funding programs—Dash, Monero, and Zcash—to understand how decentralized projects allocate money when they have it. She examines each program's structure across funding source, application process, decision-making, and accountability, finding significant design tradeoffs between centralized committees, stakeholder voting, and crowdfunding. The analysis reveals that funding primarily goes to a small group of trusted core contributors rather than random applicants, and that reputation is the strongest signal for who gets funded. She concludes that projects don't need enormous budgets to make a difference, but benefit greatly from transparent processes, pre-proposal filtering, and community stakeholder involvement in decisions.
key_points: Three distinct funding models—crowdfunded (Monero), blockchain-allocated budget (Dash), and foundation-funded grants (Zcash)—each carry meaningful tradeoffs around decentralization, sustainability, and governance
Funding concentrates heavily among a small group of trusted, repeat contributors: nearly half of Monero's funded proposals went to just six people, and over a third of Dash's to three people
Having a dedicated budget (like Dash's $1M+/month) may incentivize superfluous spending, particularly on marketing, while crowdfunded models risk unsustainability if community interest wanes
Public application processes with pre-proposal stages effectively balance openness with noise reduction, and no program participants complained about transparency
Not all valuable contributions require funding—some work like translations happens because contributors care, and some contributors build first and seek funding after demonstrating value
money_quotes: What a project spends money on depends on how much of it they have. A $1,000 budget enables very different funding decisions from a $100,000 or $1M budget, not to mention whether it's recurring or not.
If you have the money, you'll find ways to spend it, even if it's not really necessary.
Today, many full-time maintainers are hired by a company to work on the project, but in these cases, a project's community becomes its board, which seems better from a governance standpoint.
Funding is ideally allocated towards regular contributors, maintainers, and people with 'known' reputations, rather than random or first-time contributors.
In open source, not everything that's valuable is immediately fundable, but that's not necessarily a bad thing.
Leaving it too open can turn funding into a popularity contest.
I came away feeling more certain that money can unlock so much more opportunity and stability for open source projects.
posts.key_insight: Decentralized open source funding programs, regardless of their structural design, converge on directing money toward a small group of trusted repeat contributors, suggesting that reputation and demonstrated commitment matter more than the specific funding mechanism.

posts.summary: The post examines Handshake's model of giving away 70% of its value to developers as a superior alternative to traditional platform developer funds. Traditional developer funds, like those from Slack or Facebook, take equity in developers' companies in exchange for funding, which creates misaligned incentives. Handshake flips this by rewarding developers with ownership in the platform itself, aligning interests between platform and developer. The author argues this equity model could ease the inevitable friction when platforms make policy changes that hurt third-party developers, drawing on examples from Apple's App Store and Twitter's contentious developer relations. Treating developers as collaborators with a stake rather than extractable resources would benefit both sides.
key_points: Traditional platform developer funds reward developers by taking equity in their companies, which misaligns incentives since developers are already increasing the platform's value
Handshake's model of giving developers ownership in the platform itself creates better alignment between platform growth and developer reward
Platform equity for developers could soften the blow of inevitable policy changes, similar to how early employees receive stock that compensates them even as their roles diminish
Platform-developer relationships today resemble political battles rather than collaborations, as illustrated by Twitter's long contentious history with third-party developers
Developer equity is simpler and cheaper for platforms than acquiring and shutting down popular third-party apps
money_quotes: Far from altruistic, by giving away ownership to incentivize development, Handshake seems like a better version of the "developer funds" offered by platforms like Slack, Facebook, or Cloudflare.
Developers who build popular apps on a platform arguably increase the company's valuation. In exchange for this service, the platform takes equity in their company. (The gall!)
Instead of taking value from the developer, they reward them with ownership in Handshake. In hindsight, this seems like an obviously better alignment between platform and developers.
Equity awards are likely simpler and cheaper than acquiring popular apps and shutting them down.

This is not unlike an early employee whose generalist skills become less useful as the company grows. They can go on to do other things, while also feeling adequately compensated for the early work they put in.
Platform-developer relationships today resemble a political battle more than a dance.
App developers work with platforms suspiciously, giving them the side-eye instead of being excited to build cool things with it.

I don't see developer equity as a worse option for companies, but rather a way to better align incentive and reward structures, treating developers as collaborators with a stake in the company's future instead of pure value extraction.
posts.key_insight: Platforms should reward third-party developers with equity in the platform itself rather than extracting equity from developers' companies, transforming an adversarial relationship into a collaborative one.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues against the reflexive habit of declaring oneself 'not technical,' using her grandmother's fearless approach to learning German as an analogy. She contends that saying 'I'm not technical' prematurely limits conversations and dampens the enthusiasm of those trying to share their knowledge. She points out that no one is literate across all technical fields, so the distinction between technical and non-technical people is largely artificial. The real differentiator is willingness to engage, ask questions, and tolerate confusion. Through her grandmother's example of laughing at mistakes and persistently practicing, she advocates for patient immersion as a path to fluency in any domain.
key_points: Declaring 'I'm not technical' shuts down the upper bound of a conversation and signals unwillingness to engage, dampening the other person's enthusiasm
The concept of 'technical people' is largely nonsensical since no one is literate across all technical fields—a chemist doesn't understand cryptography any more than a non-specialist would
Questions are not a sign of ignorance but a sign of paying attention, and technical fluency comes from willingness to sit with confusion
Immersion and fearlessness with mistakes—as demonstrated by her grandmother learning six languages—is the real path to understanding
money_quotes: I'd rather absorb their unfiltered world than have them treat me like a tourist.
Saying "I'm not technical", while a well-intentioned reflex, prematurely shuts down the upper bound of the conversation.
It's the conversational equivalent of putting your hands up.
Questions aren't a sign of ignorance; they're a sign you're paying attention.
I think technical people are just people who try to understand.
It's okay if it feels like complete gibberish for awhile, and it's okay to admit that you only understood 5% of the conversation thus far.
Your brain is wired to find patterns in chaos. Eventually, the words will decipher themselves.
posts.key_insight: Technical fluency isn't an identity you either have or don't—it's a willingness to stay engaged through confusion, ask questions without shame, and trust that understanding accumulates over time.

posts.summary: This post examines how popular open source projects manage user support at scale, based on research into the top 100 projects by issue volume on GitHub. Nadia finds that most projects use multiple support channels beyond GitHub issues, with forums, Stack Overflow, and chat being most common. She identifies two scaling models: decentralized (community-powered) and centralized (paid) support, noting that user and maintainer motivations are inversely correlated over a project's lifecycle. The post argues that the traditional 'onion model' of open source contribution is too narrow, proposing instead a 'hub-and-spokes' model that recognizes distinct maintainer roles including code development, issue triage, and user support as equally legitimate forms of contribution.
key_points: There is no significant correlation between GitHub stars and issue volume; stars are a poor proxy for support burden
Projects receiving more than one issue per day likely need to think about scaling support, and most top projects use ~2 channels beyond GitHub issues
User support scales as a marginal cost of software adoption, unlike the common belief that software scales costlessly
The motivations of users and maintainers are inversely correlated: maintainers start eager to help but lose interest as the project grows, while user motivation to participate in support increases

The 'onion model' of open source should be replaced with a 'hub-and-spokes' model recognizing that maintainership extends beyond commit access to include issue triage, user support, and community management
money_quotes: We can't have it both ways: either people contribute because they get to do what they like, or we need to find other ways to distribute or manage the support burden.
Unlike many digital goods (like a book, or a song), software requires ongoing upkeep and maintenance.
Projects getting more than one issue a day are probably starting to think about scaling up their support needs.
We need to expand our definition of 'maintainer': issue management, user support, and writing code are distinct roles.
A code maintainer shouldn't have to answer questions all day, and a support maintainer shouldn't have to commit code, if that's not what they each want to do.
Like a coral reef, there's an entire ecosystem quietly blooming across other channels.
Before we had the issue template, despite our extensive bug reporting instructions, about 80% of the issue reports we got were useless.
Most answerers I looked at weren't involved with the GitHub repository at all, or had opened or interacted with a couple issues or PRs.
posts.key_insight: Open source maintainership is far broader than writing code, and projects thrive when they recognize user support, issue triage, and development as distinct roles with different motivations and incentive structures.

posts.summary: Nadia introduces 'Hope in Source,' a ten-episode podcast series she created with open source developer Henry Zhu exploring parallels between faith and open source communities. She observed that religious faith, particularly Christianity, was surprisingly prevalent among open source developers, contrasting with the secular culture of San Francisco tech. The podcast examines how both faith communities and open source projects share organizational patterns around financing, mythology, membership, and public service. Through recording the conversations, Nadia discovered she was less 'non-religious' than she assumed, recognizing her own spiritual practice expressed through her work amplifying creative potential. Both hosts felt vulnerable discussing religion publicly, noting that faith is a topic people in tech deliberately avoid for fear of being labeled. The project was conceived as a finite artifact for shared learning rather than an ongoing media venture.
key_points: Faith is surprisingly prevalent among open source developers, contrasting with the secular norms of mainstream tech culture in places like San Francisco
Open source and organized religion share deep structural parallels: distributed community governance, financing models, mythology and symbolism, and building lasting congregations
Discussing religion openly in tech feels risky because it appends an identity label that people fear will change how others perceive them
The podcast was designed as a finite learning artifact rather than an ongoing media product, challenging the assumption that podcasts must be perpetual
Through exploring faith intellectually, Nadia realized she had her own spiritual practice expressed through her drive to amplify creative potential in the world
money_quotes: Faith and open source both place high value on community and a sense of public service. Organized religion is the original distributed community, and it's lasted thousands of years.

They're organizational systems that bind people tightly together, and whose norms, fascinatingly, didn't derive from any one place, but rather from a shared consensus iterated upon by each passing generation.
Discussing religion can feel like appending a label to your identity, one that will make people see you in an irrevocably different way.
People here who work in tech are fiercely optimistic, and maybe even dogmatic about it, but religion is conspicuously absent from our day-to-day.
I have my own version of spiritual practice; I'm just not used to acknowledging it to others.
We wanted to challenge the idea that podcasts have to be a 'forever' thing.
People worry - rightly so - that openly discussing faith could send the wrong message or invite unwanted attention.
posts.key_insight: Organized religion and open source communities share deep structural parallels as distributed, consensus-driven systems built on public service, yet discussing faith remains taboo in tech culture.

posts.summary: Asparouhova draws a parallel between her decade of ethical vegetarianism and the current debate over whether infrastructure providers should police how their services are used. She recounts how her strict vegetarian principles kept encountering irresolvable edge cases—field mice killed by farming, animal products in tires and beer—until she concluded that perfect ethical consistency was impossible. She applies this same logic to calls for tech infrastructure providers to de-platform bad actors, arguing that the chain of moral responsibility quickly becomes absurd if extended from cloud hosting to programming languages to Gmail to food. She concludes that while technology use is subject to moral debate, where we draw the line on infrastructure responsibility is a matter of policy, not ethics.
key_points: Strict ethical frameworks inevitably encounter edge cases that reveal their internal contradictions, as demonstrated by vegetarian koans like field mice deaths from farming
The impulse to blame infrastructure providers after harmful events online mirrors the impulse to achieve moral purity through dietary choices—both are driven by a desire to regain a sense of control
Open source licenses deliberately prohibit discrimination against persons or fields of endeavor, recognizing the impossibility of attaching moral instructions to the spread of technology
The chain of infrastructure responsibility can be extended absurdly from cloud providers to programming languages to hardware to food, revealing the slippery slope of de-platforming logic
Where to draw the line on infrastructure provider responsibility is a policy question, not an ethical one
money_quotes: I realized that I was saying no not because they broke my moral framework, but because I wanted to uphold the idea of ethics-based vegetarianism. It was about maintaining my identity as a vegetarian, rather than evaluating the question on its own merit.
These stories make us feel a profound loss of control, and we process them by finding a way to believe that we could prevent anything like it from happening again.

Should Gmail ban all hateful people from using its service to communicate? Should Apple not sell them iPhones? What about the food that nourishes and sustains these people, or the clothing brands they wore in the act?
They recognized it's impossible to attach instructions to the invention and spread of technology, and they had the foresight to protect developers from liability.
Technology itself may be amoral, but what we do with technology is absolutely up for moral debate.
What starts out reasonably plausible will quickly devolve into a series of what-abouts.
Wherever we land is a matter of policy, not ethics.
posts.key_insight: Holding infrastructure providers morally responsible for how their services are used leads to the same impossible purity spiral as strict ethical vegetarianism—where we draw that line is a policy decision, not an ethical imperative.

posts.summary: Nadia argues that software foundations are a flawed governance model for open source projects because they create centralized single points of failure for what are inherently distributed endeavors. She challenges the assumption that foundations are inherently good due to their nonprofit status, noting they face less public accountability than companies. After breaking down foundation roles into legal/financial services and governance, she proposes alternatives: virtualizing legal services through fiscal sponsors like Software Freedom Conservancy or Open Collective, and replacing foundation governance with a distributed model where the project holds sovereign authority over multiple participating entities. The ideal trajectory starts with a central Entity(0) that actively works to distribute influence and contribution opportunities across the ecosystem, making itself just one actor rather than the single point of failure.
key_points: Foundations enjoy a 'free-rider' effect where their nonprofit status shields them from the scrutiny applied to companies, despite being little more than a tax designation
Software foundations bundle together distinct functions—holding assets, managing IP, education, and governance—that could be better handled by separate, specialized providers
The foundation model creates a winner-take-all monopoly that is structurally at odds with the distributed nature of open source projects
A distributed governance model where Entity(0) actively works to dilute its own centrality better aligns with how successful open source projects function as standards rather than organizations
Projects only need to formalize governance at the first sign of conflict, and many worry about it earlier than necessary
money_quotes: There is nothing about a foundation that is inherently good, neutral, or charitable, simply because they have a 501(c) status.
Foundations are, counterintuitively, subject to less public accountability than companies.
We assume foundations are default-good and don't pay as much attention to them, whereas we assume a company is default-bad and hold them to task.
The foundation, as a single point of failure, is structurally at odds with the idea of the project, which is inherently distributed.

Just as 'the government' is not 'the people', but rather a representative body whose legitimacy derives from sustained popular support, 'the foundation' is not 'the project', and should not be treated as such.
Rather than checking power with more power, the intended approach is to dilute all central nodes of influence over time, using a decentralized model.
When an open source project succeeds, it looks more like a standard than an organization, and standards succeed when they propagate.
Much like language, religion, or etiquette, successful open source projects should exist beyond the control of any one company or foundation.
posts.key_insight: Software foundations create centralized single points of failure that contradict open source's distributed nature, and projects should instead pursue governance models that actively dilute any single entity's influence over time.

posts.summary: Nadia reflects on two years of running Helium Grants, a personal microgrant program funding individuals rather than organizations. She identifies a massive funding gap in philanthropy: institutional funders can't easily give to individuals due to tax and legal structures, pushing people toward organizations even when independent work would be more productive. She draws parallels to venture capital's evolution toward seed funding and argues philanthropy needs a similar shift. After experimenting with broad, no-strings-attached giving, she learned that validation matters as much as money, narrower focus beats broad mandates, and following grantees' journeys adds meaning. She announces a pivot to focus specifically on funding independent research.
key_points: Philanthropy has a structural funding gap for individuals because US foundations can only give tax-deductible grants to 501c3 nonprofits, pushing groundbreaking work into organizational containers it doesn't need
Modern payment tools like Venmo, PayPal, and TransferWise make sending money to individuals worldwide nearly frictionless, yet philanthropic bureaucracy still creates months-long delays
Social validation and amplification often matter more to grantees than the money itself, making personal giving an investment in people rather than a simple wealth transfer
Broad 'fund anything' criteria became unfair and diluted meaning; narrowing focus to independent research better serves both the funder's authentic interests and applicants' expectations
Philanthropy needs its own version of seed funding — smaller amounts given earlier to individuals, driven by private rather than institutional giving
money_quotes: The idea that groundbreaking work is driven by individuals probably makes sense to a lot of people, yet in practice, there's no readily available funding for individuals.
Sometimes, an individual just needs a check, and a vote of confidence from someone they respect, to keep going.
It's really easy to give money to people on the internet today, and we should do more of it.
Wealth isn't really about money, but influence. Lots of people have cash to give away. Social capital may, but doesn't always, correlate to net worth.
I was surprised to learn how many applicants said that the validation mattered more than the money.
I strongly believe that social progress is powered by individuals, often outsiders, who look at intractable problems in a new light.
Groundbreaking ideas are born as a seed of madness, germinating in one person's brain.
Unlike investor accreditation, there's no minimum threshold for how rich you need to be to give privately. If you have money, and you want to support someone's work, just reach out to them and do it.
posts.key_insight: Philanthropy needs to evolve the way venture capital did — moving downstream to fund individuals early with small amounts and personal validation, not just organizations with large institutional grants.

posts.summary: Asparouhova explores the duality of human nature as caught between animal instinct and divine aspiration, arguing that neither framework adequately explains what we are. She uses the 'Dissolving the Fermi Paradox' paper as a springboard to suggest that our search for external meaning—whether through religion, science, or extraterrestrial life—may be misguided. Rather than explaining human behavior through biology or theology, she proposes we are something entirely unique: neither animal nor God. She concludes that we should examine the artifacts of human civilization inductively, working backward from what we've produced to understand why we're here, finding meaning not in cosmic explanations but in the bewildering reality of human existence itself.
key_points: Humans exist in a unique state between animal and God, capable of holding both lowbrow and highbrow impulses simultaneously, yet reducible to neither
Our successive obsessions with God, extraterrestrial life, and AI represent the same futile search for external meaning in the wake of secularization
The 'Dissolving the Fermi Paradox' paper suggests we may be alone in the universe, which should elevate our reverence for each human encounter rather than produce nihilism
Rather than deductive reasoning from biology or religion, we should use inductive reasoning—examining the artifacts and outputs of human civilization to understand ourselves
The finite number of humans playing an infinite game demands we treat each person and each action as consequential
money_quotes: This is what it means to be both animal and God: that particular human ability to hold both lowbrow and highbrow in a single state and, depending on the moment, dissemble and disavow knowledge of either one.
Our interest in extraterrestrial life is a fairly modern obsession, overlapping with a waning interest in God: a search for meaning in the wake of secularization.
Politicians don't start wars because they're territorial primates, nor as an elaborate conspiracy to increase shareholder value. They do it for reasons even they don't understand.
There is no comfort - neither religion nor science - that will save us.
Even if humanity implodes, ideas survive without a host, hovering in purgatory like space garbage.
We are playing an infinite game with a finite number of players, so we'd better learn how to play nicely, and play well.
Being human is more bewildering and beautiful than either animal or God, because the end of making meaning signifies the start of it all.
posts.key_insight: Rather than explaining humanity through the lens of biology or religion, we should embrace our unique position as neither animal nor God and examine the artifacts of civilization inductively to find meaning in what we've actually produced.

posts.summary: Asparouhova applies Jane Jacobs' framework from 'The Death and Life of Great American Cities' to understand online public life. She distinguishes between technical privacy (keeping data secure) and social privacy (the norm of not prying into others' lives). Jacobs' concept of 'sidewalk life' — meaningful public interactions between strangers who respect each other's boundaries — maps directly onto social platforms like Twitter and Facebook. Healthy sidewalk life requires mutual trust, public characters who facilitate connections without violating privacy, and local context. When sidewalk life breaks down, people retreat to private circles, losing the serendipity and democratic mixing that public life offers. The essay argues we must reclaim online public life rather than abandon it for private enclaves.
key_points: Privacy is not just a technical problem (encryption, data security) but a social norm — the expectation that others shouldn't want to pry, even when they technically can
Jacobs' 'sidewalk life' — public interactions between strangers who respect mutual boundaries — is the key to thriving cities and maps directly onto social media platforms
Public characters (storekeepers, social media figures) serve as trusted nodes who facilitate community without violating personal boundaries, and they appoint themselves
When public life breaks down into 'togetherness or nothing,' people retreat to private cliques, losing the serendipity, democracy, and opportunity of urban encounters
Resurfacing old social media posts illustrates the tension between technical publicity and social privacy — data being public doesn't make snooping socially acceptable
money_quotes: Privacy is about more than who can peek into your life; it's a social norm about who should.

Unlike our physical environment, our online world contains layers of our past, present, and future selves, all occupying the same timespace. We are all time travelers, navigating multiple realities at any given moment.
If our smaller online communities (like private group chats or messaging threads) are our towns, social platforms (like Facebook, Twitter, or Instagram) are our cities.
Such relationships can, and do, endure for many years, for decades; they could never have formed without that line, much less endured.
We know that he combines a feeling of good will with a feeling of no personal responsibility about our private affairs.
I have lost the advantage of living in the city without getting the advantages of living in the suburbs.
It solves easy problems for an easy kind of population. So far as I have been able to discover, it fails to work, however, even on its own terms, with any other kind of population.
We can reclaim a public life where ideas are freely shared with one another, while our need for privacy is respected.
posts.key_insight: Healthy online public life requires not just technical privacy protections but social privacy norms — the shared expectation that we won't pry into each other's lives — which enable the 'sidewalk life' of meaningful exchanges between strangers.

posts.summary: The post argues that 'shamelessness' has become a dominant strategy in public life, using the board game Avalon as a metaphor for how blatantly defying expectations can be a winning move. Asparouhova traces this from Paris Hilton's deliberately playing into the 'dumb blonde' stereotype to the 2016 U.S. presidential election, arguing that people who break social norms are rewarded rather than punished in an era of open online communities. She explains that sanctions against norm-breaking behavior used to work in bounded communities but now backfire by amplifying the transgressor's signal to outsiders. The post concludes that dismissing shameless actors as stupid is itself a mistake, and that the strategy works regardless of whether the person is a conscious mastermind.
key_points: In bounded communities, social sanctions effectively punish norm-breaking behavior, but in open online communities with fluid borders, punishment only amplifies the transgressor's reach
People dismiss shameless strategists because acknowledging their success would mean admitting they themselves have been playing an inferior game
The shameless person functions as a host for ideas that propagate virally — whether they are a 'genius mastermind' or not is irrelevant to the strategy's effectiveness
Paris Hilton pioneered the modern shamelessness playbook that later powered the Kardashians, celebrity socialites, and arguably the 2016 U.S. election
The strategy works by creating deliberate confusion about whether the actor is being incredibly stupid or incredibly smart
money_quotes: The evil team, believing that no Merlin would be stupid enough to put himself out there like that, figures he must be Percival, and writes him off.
People were dismissive of Paris because validating her playbook would mean admitting that they were playing an inferior game.
Today, it seems like punishing shamelessness only increases social rewards to the transgressor.
Under open borders, sanctions will backfire, because they just serve as a signaling boost for the transgressor, attracting outsiders who resonate with that person's message.
What's meant to be punishment instead becomes a flare shot straight into the night sky.
The disgust of one's peers doesn't matter anymore, because that disgust forms the basis for an entirely new community.
The concept of a 'genius mastermind' is itself outdated, because it assumes that someone needs to be in control.
The shameless person is simply a host for a set of ideas, which, like any virus, will continue to propagate as long as there are willing hosts to receive it.
posts.key_insight: In a world of fluid online communities, social sanctions against norm-breaking behavior backfire by amplifying the transgressor's signal to new audiences, turning shamelessness from a liability into a dominant strategy.

posts.summary: Nadia argues that new open source licenses are unlikely to solve the maintainer compensation problem, both for practical and philosophical reasons. On the practical side, even GPL—the most established restrictive license—is routinely ignored by users and companies, with mixed legal enforcement and developer ambivalence about pursuing violations. On the philosophical side, she believes the focus should shift from controlling consumption (tollbooths on code access) to capturing value on the production side—maintainer attention, access, and expertise. She advocates for patronage, sponsorships, and membership models rather than legal mechanisms, arguing that information once freely shared cannot be effectively re-enclosed.
key_points: GPL's decades of real-world experience shows that restrictive licenses are routinely ignored, weakly enforced, and sometimes actively avoided—casting doubt on any new license's ability to succeed where GPL hasn't
Recent relicensing attempts by companies like MongoDB, Redis, and React faced strong community opposition, and individual maintainers face even harsher personal backlash

Free-riding on code consumption isn't actually the core problem—most users never impose costs on maintainers; costs come from the minority who interact with the production side (bugs, questions, patches)
The real opportunity lies in pricing maintainer attention and access rather than code access, through patronage, sponsorships, and membership models
Trying to control information distribution through licenses is a pre-digital solution to modern problems that cannot realistically be re-imposed
money_quotes: Given that after several decades in the wild, it still seems to be 'look the other way' in practice, I'm not particularly convinced that your new license will be any different.
While everyone is focused on putting up tollbooths, opportunities to 'price' maintainer attention, and access to maintainers, remain undervalued.
Most users don't actually impose costs back to maintainers: they download the code and never interact with the project.
Information, once freely shared, is a Pandora's box that cannot be closed again.
It is very hard for me to imagine a world where we transition back to paying for access to information; these are pre-digital solutions to modern problems.
Instead of trying to stuff jack back into the box, let code run wild.
I tend to view it as a coping mechanism, distracting us from the uncomfortable realization - perhaps felt collectively today - that regulation can't really rein in the internet after all.
If we start from scratch with a fresh set of assumptions, we have the opportunity to come up with radically different solutions.
posts.key_insight: Open source sustainability efforts should stop trying to control who consumes code and instead focus on capturing value from the production side—maintainer attention, access, and expertise—because information once freed cannot be effectively re-enclosed.

posts.summary: Nadia argues that the patronage model for creators has been distorted by Kickstarter's blueprint of reward tiers and transactional perks, when what patrons actually want is intimacy and ritual. Drawing from her own experience transforming her newsletter from brief updates into rich personal dispatches, she found that giving more of herself—not less—deepened subscriber engagement. Using The Little Prince's concept of 'taming' through rituals, she explains that people follow creators not for material rewards but because regular contact creates meaningful bonds. She contends that creators sell intimacy to patrons and 'stuff' to customers, and that the industry should redesign patronage tools around relationships rather than transactions.
key_points: Nadia's newsletter engagement improved dramatically when she shifted from transactional updates to sharing her full world and interests, proving subscribers wanted intimacy not information
The Little Prince's concept of 'taming' through rituals explains why people form irrational loyalty to creators—regular contact creates unique bonds regardless of the content's objective value

Kickstarter functioned as 'original sin' for patronage platforms, establishing a blueprint of reward tiers and perks that every subsequent platform copied without questioning whether it actually served creators
The distinction between patrons and customers is that patrons pay for ritual and relationship while customers pay for stuff and perks
Modern patronage tools should be redesigned around the principle that genuine supporters don't need to be bribed to stick around
money_quotes: It turns out that the people who followed my work didn't really want 'updates'. In fact, I'm starting to suspect that people have no idea what I actually do for a living (and I kind of like it that way!).
Whether it's a newsletter, podcast, or social platform, people follow other people for reasons that are often incomprehensible, even to themselves. Somehow, your life became a ritual to them.

It's not that I or anyone else is really that gosh darn interesting. Rather, there are so many people out there to look at, and so many people they can reach, it's inevitable any one will be able to draw a small crowd.
Patrons pay for the ritual, and the ritual tames them. These are ongoing relationships, not transactions. Creators sell intimacy to patrons. They sell 'stuff' - perks - to customers.
Sometimes I think of Kickstarter as the original sin for modern patronage.
Reward tiers are an awkward thing for creators and their patrons. People don't necessarily need material perks. They just want to feel close to that person.
Like any other relationship, if someone loves and cares about your work, you don't need to bribe them to stick around.
posts.key_insight: Patronage is fundamentally about ritual and intimacy rather than transactional perks, and the creator economy's tools should be redesigned around relationships instead of copying Kickstarter's reward-tier blueprint.

posts.summary: Nadia shares practical advice from running Helium Grants, a personal microgrant program she operated for two years, processing ~4,000 applications and awarding nearly $40,000 to 26 people. She covers logistics like managing applications via Google Forms, batching review cycles quarterly, and leveraging sponsors who contributed most of the funding. The more substantive lessons concern program design: treating grantmaking as an investment rather than a giveaway, curating grantees as a portfolio, and finding the right balance of specificity in funding criteria. She emphasizes that the mental overhead of responsibility was harder than the actual hours required. The post serves as a practical cheat sheet for anyone considering starting their own microgrant program.
key_points: Keep logistics minimal: use Google Forms, batch application reviews quarterly, and avoid forming a legal entity if the amounts are small enough to qualify as personal gifts
Treat grantmaking as an investment, not a giveaway — validation and social capital matter more to grantees than the money itself, and 'dumb money' programs die from neglect
Think of grantees as a portfolio and cohort: curate for the story and community you want to build, not just individual merit
Find the right level of specificity — too broad makes selection impossible, but overly specific criteria causes applicants to perform rather than reveal themselves authentically
Most funding came from external sponsors rather than personal money, creating a win-win where sponsors learned about grantmaking through an existing platform
money_quotes: Treat grantmaking as an investment, not a giveaway.
A number of applicants wrote that the validation mattered more to them than the money.
Returns are measured by social capital: if a grantee does something admirable or interesting in the world, they make you look good.
Evaluate applications not just based on individual merit, but by the story you want to tell about the grant program more generally.
While it's exciting to go broad, the lack of criteria makes it harder to choose applicants.

If you say you're looking for a 'motivated self-starter', people will tailor their applications to look like motivated self-starters, but what you're really looking for are exceptional candidates whose abilities are evident by reading between the lines.
While managing the program itself didn't require a lot of hours, it felt like a lot of responsibility, which translated into mental overhead.
posts.key_insight: Effective microgrant programs succeed not by writing checks but by treating grantmaking as an investment — curating a portfolio of grantees, building community among them, and recognizing that validation and social capital matter more than the money itself.

posts.summary: The post reframes the relationship between creators and their ideas, arguing that ideas function as parasitic entities that ride people rather than the reverse. Drawing on China Miéville's Embassytown, Asparouhova proposes a 'great prophet theory' where creators are vessels for idea-viruses rather than autonomous agents. She examines how reputation binds creators to their ideas like a devil's contract, and how the internet has commodified creative output while making individual creators more interchangeable. The essay concludes by exploring three strategies creators use to resist being consumed: resisting definition entirely, compartmentalizing through alts and pseudonyms, or accepting ideas as separate entities that can shield the creator from public scrutiny.
key_points: Ideas function as parasitic entities that seize creators as hosts, rather than creators being autonomous agents of change — a 'great prophet theory' inverting the 'great man theory'
Reputation is a devil's contract: creators are rewarded for continuing to produce the same type of work but punished for deviation, as audiences own the idea more than the creator does
The internet has made individual creators more interchangeable — audiences seek specific sounds, aesthetics, or ideas rather than specific people, commodifying creators through algorithmic discovery

Creators can resist being consumed by their ideas through three strategies: resisting definition entirely (like Kanye), compartmentalizing via pseudonyms and alts, or strategically managing a portfolio of ideas
When we mourn exceptional artists, we mourn the scarcity of their product rather than the person — we crave a sound or style that feels irreplaceable
money_quotes: Rather than viewing people as agents of change, I think of them as intermediaries, voice boxes for some persistent idea-virus that's seized upon them and is speaking through their corporeal form.
Ideas ride us into battle like warhorses. We can witness, participate in, and even lead these battles, but their true meaning eludes us.
The creator must battle their own idea in order to survive, taming it back into submission, or risk being subsumed by its demands.
Reputation is the aggregation of ideas that have swarmed your body. Reputation is a list of your chronic afflictions.
Continue to make the same thing, and you'll be rewarded, but don't stray too far from the original concept.
When someone says 'Nobody ever played guitar like Jimi Hendrix', they're saying that they crave a sound that feels hard to find elsewhere, not that they miss Jimi Hendrix himself.
Spotify Discover Weekly satisfies the consumer by commodifying the creator.
Ideas are fascinators that sparkle and dangle in front of the creator, distracting an eager audience from the person behind the curtain.
posts.key_insight: Ideas are parasitic entities that use creators as hosts, and reputation is the binding contract that locks creators into serving their audience's addiction to a particular idea — making the creator interchangeable but the idea indispensable.

posts.summary: Nadia explores how GitHub Sponsors could succeed as a user-to-user product by drawing parallels to Twitch streaming dynamics. She challenges the conventional wisdom that corporate sponsorships are the only viable funding path for open source, arguing that individual developers follow and fund other developers for fundamentally different reasons than companies do. By comparing open source to 'high-latency streaming,' she identifies education and mastery as shared motivations between Twitch viewers and GitHub followers. She concludes that individual and corporate sponsorships should be treated as two distinct products serving different needs, with individuals drawn to the developer's process and persona rather than just their code output.
key_points: GitHub's follow feature reveals an underappreciated social dynamic where less prominent developers enthusiastically follow the work of developers they admire, motivated by learning and watching experts work
Open source development is a form of 'high-latency streaming' — developers gain followers because people enjoy watching them do something in public, similar to Twitch streamers

Individual and corporate sponsorships serve fundamentally different motivations: individuals want to learn, watch, and connect with developers they respect, while companies want support, security, and brand visibility
GitHub Sponsors for individuals could function more like Twitch channel subscriptions than Patreon patronage, because the audience is motivated by process and education, not just gratitude for output
Revenue is not zero-sum — some developers and projects will be better suited to corporate sponsorships while others thrive on individual subscriptions
money_quotes: Open source is a sort of 'high-latency streaming'. It's streaming, but in staccato snapshots.
With companies, open source developers are selling a product. With individuals, they're selling themselves.
Although people probably watch Twitch streamers in part because they enjoy the game that's being played, it seems they're more attracted to the streamer's process than their output.
Individual sponsors are probably more likely to fund crazy new ideas, whereas companies are probably more inclined to support critical, existing projects.

Rather than individual sponsorships being a path to company sponsorships, the motivations of individuals are different from companies, and therefore should be treated as two separate use cases, and two separate products.

There are individuals who fund open source developers as a donation or a thank you for their work, but the total addressable market will always feel small, compared to what companies can offer. But if we frame individual sponsorships in terms of social dynamics, it implies a different sort of economy altogether.

Encouraging developers to follow and subscribe to other developers feels uniquely GitHub to me, in a way that, say, Patreon couldn't really do, because GitHub is the platform where open source is built.
posts.key_insight: Individual sponsorships of open source developers should not be seen as a stepping stone to corporate funding but as a distinct economy driven by social dynamics of learning and admiration, analogous to Twitch subscriptions rather than Patreon patronage.

posts.summary: The post explores the tension between sharing discoveries about hidden communities and protecting those communities from unwanted attention. Drawing parallels between physical places and online subcultures, Asparouhova argues that online communities are especially vulnerable because their 'product' is the ideas generated by members, and an influx of outsiders can damage the incubative environment. She contrasts passive consumption (like eating croissants at a bakery) with the participatory nature of online communities, where the line between consumer and producer is blurred. She concludes that while change is inevitable and even desirable, being thoughtful about the rate of change matters, and lurking respectfully is an underappreciated option.
key_points: Online communities are more vulnerable to outside attention than physical places because mobility is frictionless—visiting requires only a click, not a plane ticket
The 'product' of online communities is a function of who participates, unlike a bakery where customers passively consume—flooding a community with newcomers can destroy its creative environment
Community hazing and resistance to labels are adaptive mechanisms for preserving identity, not mere hostility toward outsiders
Invisible tourism is possible online: you can consume and appreciate a community's output without interfering with its dynamics
The key question is not whether to embrace change but how to be thoughtful about the rate of change introduced to fragile ecosystems
money_quotes: Avoiding labels is a way of keeping hidden cities away from the colonizers, like a nomadic tribe on the move.
Blasting a fragile ecosystem with a firehose of outsiders, even if they have good intentions, can destroy its essence.
Online communities are even more vulnerable to outside attention than physical cities, because mobility is so much easier.
Croissants are passively consumed by paying customers; nobody is jumping over the counter and trying to make the croissants themselves.
Too much unwanted attention and catering to newcomers can detract from, and ultimately damage, the incubative state of ideas.

One tweet, post, or article from someone with a big enough audience is like sunlight on a magnifying glass, concentrating screaming hordes of people onto a single, unsuspecting point that spontaneously combusts into flames.
You can visit an online community, can enjoy reading through the interactions of people you might otherwise never get to know, without interfering with their magic.
When does enthusiasm cross over to exploitation? I'm not sure I know the difference yet, but I know that there is one.
posts.key_insight: Online communities are uniquely fragile ecosystems where the line between appreciating and destroying what makes them special is dangerously thin, making the rate of outside attention as important as the attention itself.

posts.summary: Asparouhova reflects on the exhaustion of living in Silicon Valley's 'idea economy,' where intellectual exchange has become labor rather than leisure. She describes the constant pressure to externalize thoughts, trade insights, and perform intellectual engagement as a form of mining. As a coping mechanism, she embraces 'being basic' — deliberately seeking anti-intellectual, simple pleasures to counterbalance the cognitive demands of her work. She speculates that as idea production becomes increasingly associated with work rather than freedom, basic behavior could become a status symbol, much as tanned skin shifted from signaling manual labor to signaling leisure.
key_points: Silicon Valley's culture of constant intellectual exchange transforms thinking and sharing ideas from leisure into exhausting labor
The pressure to externalize thoughts prematurely undermines the slow, organic creative process that produces genuine insight
Being 'basic' is distinct from being mediocre or degenerate — it represents a total denial of awareness of deeper intellectual currents, a state of innocence
Status signals may invert: as idea production becomes work, signaling how little you think could become more prestigious than signaling how much you think
The fantasy of 'information suicide' reflects a real tension between creative work and the performative demands of intellectual communities
money_quotes: Silicon Valley is a coal mine for ideas.

Our words are filled with whimsy – after all, isn't learning so much fun?! Don't you just love to think?! – but our faces are smeared with coal dust, our eyes somewhat dulled by the knowledge that we've done this many times before.
We're like Scarlett O'Hara in Gone With the Wind: our gowns are impressively fashioned from velvet curtains, but our hands are still rough from the farm.
Sometimes it feels like I can't think in here, because people are constantly asking me to externalize my thoughts all the time.

I'm not sure it's that I want to disappear from the internet, but just to get some distance between me and the existential 'publish or perish' treadmill of mining each others' brains for pithy insights that fit into 280 characters.
Why would anyone want to have a deep, meaningful discussion all the time?
Being basic is a state of innocence and simplicity, Adam and Eve before the apple.
When I'm drinking mango White Claw and laughing in the dappled sunshine, I'm projecting a version of myself to the world that says, 'I haven't had to think a day in my life.'
posts.key_insight: When intellectual exchange becomes labor rather than leisure, deliberately embracing simplicity and anti-intellectualism may evolve from private coping mechanism to public status symbol.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova reflects on her four-plus years of independent research into open source software economics, describing how she unintentionally constructed her own version of a PhD program. She outlines three phases: discovery (funded by Ford Foundation grants, building relationships and publishing a foundational report), testing (working at GitHub to validate hypotheses through hands-on product experience), and refining (synthesizing everything into a book at Protocol Labs). She argues that independent research can be a viable alternative to a traditional PhD, particularly when the researcher's goal is to influence public discourse and reach practitioners rather than academics. The essay provides practical advice for aspiring independent researchers, including working in public, building support networks, and creating artifacts suited to your audience rather than conforming to academic norms.
key_points: Independent research can replicate the value of a PhD when structured with intentional phases: discovery, hypothesis testing through hands-on experience, and synthesis into a substantial artifact
Being an outsider to open source was an advantage rather than a liability, enabling pattern recognition across projects that insiders embedded in one ecosystem couldn't achieve
The combination of theoretical research and practical hands-on experience (working at GitHub) was far more powerful than either alone — rhetoric had to meet reality
Funding for independent research is inherently gig-based and uncertain, but writing thoughtfully in public about niche topics reliably attracts sponsors and opportunities
The isolation of long-form synthesis work is the most emotionally grueling phase of research, and building accountability structures and support networks is essential to survive it
money_quotes: Most open source developers only know their own experience, which is often limited to one project or one language ecosystem. But as an outsider, I could look for patterns across lots of different projects.
Knowing that someone cares about what you're doing is one of those things that seems totally trivial unless you're currently in that flailing-about sort of situation.
I was forced to make rhetoric meet reality.
Don't just research for the sake of research, or soon you'll just feel like a person who reads all day (which might sound cool, but I promise is actually soul-crushing).
If I want to cite a developer's tweet or Taylor Swift lyrics instead of an academic paper, I can just do that.
You're hopping from place to place, living in mortal fear of the day that some CFO type starts to run the numbers and wonder what the heck you're doing there.
When I think about alternatives to a PhD, I imagine combining the best of both worlds: injecting theoretical concepts directly into the veins of practitioners.
Independent research affords an incredible amount of freedom, but getting a blank check can be surprisingly scary.
posts.key_insight: A self-directed research career that cycles between theoretical exploration and hands-on practical experience can rival a traditional PhD in rigor and exceed it in real-world impact, provided the researcher builds accountability structures, works in public, and stays close to their subject matter.

posts.summary: Nadia explores the idea of organized voter abstention as a legitimate form of political protest, pushing back against the reflexive moral outrage that greets any suggestion of not voting. She recounts proposing a mass abstention campaign to politically-minded peers and being met with derision. Drawing on Hirschman's exit/voice framework, she argues that abstention represents a valid 'exit' response to deteriorating political choices. She contends that society's infatuation with voting as the floor of civic engagement prevents honest examination of the voting system's structural flaws. While not personally advocating against voting, she calls for not-voting to be accepted as a legitimate tool of peaceful protest alongside other radical democratic proposals.
key_points: Organized voter abstention can be a deliberate political stance signifying dissatisfaction with the system, not merely laziness or apathy
Using Hirschman's exit/voice framework, abstention represents a valid 'exit' option that society refuses to acknowledge as legitimate civic engagement
