The universal push to encourage voting, while appearing nonpartisan, actually discourages critical examination of the voting system itself
Not-voting provokes disproportionate moral outrage compared to equally radical proposals like democratic lotteries or liquid democracy
Treating voting as the minimum bar for civic engagement sets an artificial threshold that forecloses deeper discussion of political infrastructure
money_quotes: Citizens could stage a walkout from elections, like going on strike but from their own country.
Characterizing abstention as apathy or laziness, rather than a form of civic engagement that rightfully probes at our ability to 'exit,' seems uncharitable.
I worry that our infatuation with voting sets the bar for civic engagement at an artificial place, which makes it difficult to discuss our political systems more comprehensively.
While asking people to vote might be nonpartisan on a per-election level, it discourages us from critically examining the system of voting itself, which has known problems, especially at scale.
If not during an election cycle, while voting is top of mind for everyone, when is the right time to discuss these flaws?
The idea of not voting seems to provoke a sort of disgust and moral outrage that, say, democratic lotteries or liquid democracy, does not, even though these are arguably equally radical proposals.
Without leaving the problem space open for honest inquiry and exploration, it's hard to imagine how our political systems—not just the players, but its underlying infrastructure—will evolve.
posts.key_insight: Society's reflexive moral outrage at voter abstention forecloses honest examination of the voting system's structural flaws, making it harder to evolve our political infrastructure beyond its current form.

posts.summary: Nadia examines Arizona State University's radical reform under President Michael Crow, who rejected the Ivy League playbook of prestige-through-exclusivity in favor of a model measured by inclusion and student success. ASU reorganized into three pillars—Academic Enterprise (access and graduation), Knowledge Enterprise (use-inspired research), and Learning Enterprise (lifelong learning beyond degrees)—while climbing into the top 100 global research universities. Crow and Dabars argue against the 'Harvardization' of universities and the isomorphism that makes institutions converge on a generic model. Nadia connects ASU's pragmatic, entrepreneurial culture to tech values she admires, while arguing that tech still needs academia as civilization's primary infrastructure for knowledge production. She concludes that ASU demonstrates prestige need not correlate with exclusivity, and that longstanding institutions can reinvent themselves.
key_points: ASU rejected prestige-through-exclusivity, accepting 86% of applicants while climbing to top-100 research university status and improving graduation rates

Universities serve three incompatible functions—teaching, research, and career preparation—and ASU's reorganization into Academic, Knowledge, and Learning Enterprises makes these distinctions explicit
Tech and academia should be allies rather than adversaries, as academia remains civilization's primary professional sector dedicated to knowledge production
ASU's 'use-inspired research' philosophy prioritizes pragmatic, real-world impact over pure specialization, including consolidating academic departments to encourage interdisciplinary problem-solving
College alternatives like bootcamps serve a specific hiring gap but don't replace the broader benefits of a liberal arts education, especially for socioeconomically disadvantaged students
money_quotes: ASU is a comprehensive public research university, measured not by whom we exclude, but rather by whom we include and how they succeed.
Love it or hate it, academia is the closest we've ever come to having a multigenerational professional sector dedicated to the production of knowledge.

Academic social networks are some of the most refined and valuable structures our civilization has produced. Nowhere have we amassed a greater concentration of specialists focused full-time on knowledge production.
It's as if hungry customers were banging down the door of the French Laundry, whose owners throw up their hands and say 'Sorry, we don't have any reservations available!'
Here, the only mistake a dean can make, the biggest mistake, is not taking risks.
Prestige doesn't have to correlate to exclusivity; longstanding institutions are capable of reinventing themselves.
There's a reason why I prefer being in tech: everything moves faster, and people actually care about making ideas happen, not just talking about them.
They couldn't win the prestige game by competing directly against the Ivies, nor did they want to become a Generic Public University, so they made up a new status game.
posts.key_insight: ASU demonstrates that universities can dramatically improve both research quality and educational access simultaneously by rejecting the exclusivity-as-prestige model and instead measuring success by whom they include and how those students succeed.

posts.summary: Nadia questions whether the creator economy is fulfilling its original promise of enabling meaningful creative work, or whether it is devolving into an entertainment-driven treadmill. She draws parallels between the 'publish or perish' pressure in academia and the constant content demands placed on creators, arguing that the obligation to produce regularly dilutes quality and purpose. She introduces the concept of a 'creator industrial complex,' analogous to the nonprofit industrial complex, where preserving the creator identity becomes more important than the work itself. She concludes that without deeper purpose, the creator economy risks being merely Entertainment 2.0 — an incremental improvement over the old media system, not a true cultural renaissance.
key_points: The creator economy emerged with little moral inquiry into what endless content production means for culture and individual purpose
There is a critical distinction between creation-as-research (purposeful, directed toward understanding) and creation-as-entertainment (undirected, self-sustaining)
A 'creator industrial complex' emerges when the obligation to maintain a creator identity overrides the original creative mission, paralleling the nonprofit industrial complex
The tools enabling creators should unlock experimentation and big projects, not just sustain content production as an end in itself
If the creator renaissance only produces Entertainment 2.0 with more individual agency, it represents incremental improvement rather than a paradigm shift
money_quotes: The 'creator economy' popped into our vernacular with surprisingly little moral inquiry.
Once we've addressed a creator's financial needs, I find myself turning my attention to purpose and meaning.
If you're reading books to try to understand something that no one else understands yet, that's research. But if you're reading books without any goal in mind, that's entertainment.
What starts out as a worthy endeavor eventually becomes about self-preservation of the ideal.
Content is a means to an end, but it shouldn't be the end goal.
The 'publish or perish' model that nudges people to rack up more followers is not the pinnacle of creative freedom; it's indentured spiritual servitude.
Without any deeper purpose in mind, aspiring to be a 'creator,' as a career move, is almost tautologically devoid of cultural meaning and impact.
If Entertainment 2.0 is the best thing that comes out of the creator renaissance, that feels like an incremental improvement, not a paradigm shift.
posts.key_insight: The creator economy risks becoming a 'creator industrial complex' where the pressure to constantly produce content transforms a potentially liberating movement into purposeless entertainment, betraying its original promise of meaningful creative freedom.

posts.summary: The post traces the evolution of science funding in tech from 2011 to 2021, identifying three distinct phases: startup-driven approaches (2011-2014), early philanthropic experiments (2015-2017), and coordinated field-building with new institutions (2018-2021). Asparouhova argues that while the problems in science funding have remained constant—slow bureaucratic processes, misaligned incentive structures, and disadvantages for early-career scientists—the approaches have diversified significantly. She identifies key catalysts for the current wave of science initiatives: COVID-19, deliberate field-building efforts like progress studies, better coordination among funders, and the crypto wealth boom. The post concludes by contrasting 'trad tech' approaches (creating new institutions with familiar structures) against 'crypto-native' approaches (inventing entirely new incentive systems), noting that the latter is riskier but potentially transformative if successful.
key_points: Science funding in tech evolved through three phases: startups as the primary vehicle (2011-2014), early philanthropy experimenting with non-startup models (2015-2017), and coordinated field-building with diverse institutional experiments (2018-2021)

The tech-native approach to science is characterized by three values: focus on supporting top talent, emphasis on bringing research to market, and a belief that change must come from outside existing institutions
COVID-19, the crypto wealth boom, deliberate field-building (op-eds, workshops, progress studies community), and better funder coordination catalyzed the current proliferation of science initiatives

Private science funders face the '$100B problem'—their capital is tiny compared to federal R&D spending—so they aim to demonstrate what's possible and inspire systemic change rather than compete dollar-for-dollar

Crypto-native 'DeSci' approaches differ fundamentally from trad tech by seeking to invent new incentive systems rather than creating new institutions with familiar structures, taking an open-source rather than Coasean approach to talent
money_quotes: Startups aren't ideal for some kinds of innovation—for example, work that requires a very long time horizon, seeks to answer very open-ended questions, or develops technology that shouldn't be owned by any one company.
We were promised flying cars, instead we got 140 characters.
The system is broken somehow….The agencies responsible for funding most scientific research generally don't encourage scientists to pursue their boldest ideas, so we don't get ambitious science.
Science's problems come from manmade systems of incentives, not from fundamental truths of the universe.
Unless we invent new games, nothing is going to change.

The tech story maps quite predictably to philanthropic efforts in previous decades, which is to say that it has a higher likelihood for success. The crypto story is radically different, requiring us to reimagine what it means to fund and develop public goods from an entirely new set of assumptions.
It is much likelier to fail, or to succeed only in limited contexts. But if it does succeed, the upside is unimaginably larger.
Science progress is a coordination problem.
posts.key_insight: The diversification of science funding approaches in tech—from startups alone to a mix of philanthropy, new institutions, and crypto-native experiments—reflects a maturing ecosystem where the most radical potential lies not in creating better institutions but in inventing entirely new incentive systems for producing public goods.

posts.summary: Asparouhova introduces the concept of 'Idea Machines' — self-sustaining networks of ideology, community, funders, and operators that transform ideas into real-world outcomes. She uses effective altruism as the strongest existing example, while arguing that EA alone cannot fulfill philanthropy's inherently pluralistic purpose. The essay maps the anatomy of an idea machine (ideology → community → agenda → funding → outcomes) and applies this framework to emerging examples like progress studies, Schmidt Futures, and crypto public goods funding. She traces the historical evolution from 20th-century foundations to today's decentralized, narrative-driven machines, arguing that the recent explosion of tech and crypto wealth has created a more liquid 'idea marketplace.' The core argument is that we need many more idea machines, not fewer, so that every good idea can find capital and operational talent to bring it to life.
key_points: Effective altruism is the best existing example of an Idea Machine, but its moral rigidity limits it to a 'club' community that will never become the dominant philanthropic paradigm
An Idea Machine requires five components: a distinct ideology, a community, a clear agenda, major funders, and operators who drive initiatives toward outcomes
Modern idea machines are decentralized and narrative-driven, evolving away from the 20th-century foundation model that was hamstrung by the 1969 Tax Reform Act
The explosion of tech and crypto wealth has made 'idea capital' cheaper and more distributed, enabling a more pluralistic marketplace of philanthropic approaches
Philanthropy is inherently pluralistic — like startups and media — and treating it as a single optimization problem misunderstands its expressive and instrumental dual nature
money_quotes: An Idea Machine is a self-sustaining organism that contains all the parts needed to turn ideas into outcomes.
If you're pro-pluralism in startups, you should also be pro-pluralism in philanthropy.
Communities need agendas to become idea machines; otherwise, they're just a group of likeminded people, without a directed purpose.

If we were to take EA literally, we'd be saying that there is an objectively best way to accomplish these outcomes, and that that way is discoverable: that complex social problems are a finite, solvable game.
As a private citizen, renouncing my right to expressive value, in favor of donating to wherever GiveWell tells me to, feels like I might as well just pay more taxes to the government.
The federal regulation of foundations didn't mean the death of idea machines. It just meant that foundations were no longer the best place to house them.
Until recently, idea capital in tech was constrained, and mostly only accessible by startup founders.
It certainly beats a world in which there are many good ideas, but no capital available to transform them into outcomes.
posts.key_insight: Philanthropy needs a pluralistic ecosystem of 'idea machines' — decentralized networks coupling ideology, community, and capital — rather than a single dominant approach like effective altruism, to effectively translate good ideas into real-world outcomes.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that a new form of antinatalism is emerging not from deliberate philosophical inquiry, but as a byproduct of social movements like climate activism, effective altruism, and AGI/x-risk concern. These movements share the implicit assumption that children are a cost rather than an asset, which she traces to a deeper issue: a lack of belief in personal agency. She contrasts this with pronatalist movements that maintain faith in humanity's ability to shape its circumstances. Rather than countering antinatalism with individualist or biological arguments, she proposes that cultivating a sense of personal agency—teaching people that the world is programmable and shaped by human action—is the most effective societal response. She suggests an emerging 'agentic divide' may define the next generation.
key_points: A new strain of antinatalism is spreading passively through social movements (climate, EA, AGI/x-risk) rather than arising from deliberate personal ethical reasoning
The common thread in antinatalist-leaning movements is portraying children as costs or victims, rooted in a belief that individuals lack the power to meaningfully influence global challenges
Neither optimism/pessimism nor individualism/collectivism adequately explains the divide between pronatalist and antinatalist movements—agency does
Teaching agency through coding, games, and social engineering may be the defining educational challenge for the next generation, replacing 'grit' as the trait du jour
Effective pronatalist counterarguments must operate at the societal level, not merely appeal to individual desires or biological instincts
money_quotes: The world doesn't happen to us; it is shaped by us.
The opposite of agency is learned helplessness.
If you believe that the world's problems are solved by people, then having children doesn't seem like a waste of resources; it seems, in fact, like the most good you could do in the world.
It's not quite pessimism that's creeping into our consciousness like a cold bony hand, but rather the insidious belief that we are helpless to do anything to change the state of the world.
Antinatalism, as a byproduct of collectively-held social beliefs, feels deeply wrong to me somehow, like it fails a basic test of humanity.
If Gen X and Millennials grew up with a 'digital divide,' perhaps Gen Z will face an 'agentic divide': those who believe they have the power to change their circumstances, versus those who do not.
Seeding the next generation of capable minds is humanity's only hope for survival and flourishing.
Instead of responding to antinatalist arguments with thinly-cloaked shaming and appeals to the 'natural' self, I think it'd be more effective to respond by teaching people a sense of personal agency.
posts.key_insight: The critical divide between pronatalist and antinatalist social movements is not optimism versus pessimism, but whether people believe they have the agency to shape the world's future.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova argues that the climate field has evolved far beyond a simple binary question of belief into a pluralistic landscape of competing tribes, each with distinct values, strategies, and visions. Through extensive research, she identifies seven climate tribes — from energy maximalists to doomers — that differ on fundamental questions like growth vs. scarcity, technology vs. regulation, and individualism vs. collectivism. She finds that people who actually work in climate are surprisingly optimistic, not doomerish, and that 'switchers' from tech and finance bring existing skills and worldviews that shape which tribe they join. The essay also introduces the broader concept of 'doomer industries' — talent ecosystems that form around perceived civilizational threats — and examines why climate, AI safety, and similar fields attract talent in ways that other important causes do not.
key_points: Climate has progressed from science to evangelism to a solutions phase characterized by competing tribes with distinct values, not a monolithic movement
People who actually work in climate tend to be optimistic problem-solvers, not doomers — the doomer narrative is largely an exterior driven by media and outsiders
Climate workers split into 'incumbents' (specialists driven by curiosity) and 'switchers' (generalists seeking meaningful second acts), with switchers' prior careers shaping which tribe they join

The seven climate tribes — energy maximalism, climate urbanism, climate tech, eco-globalism, environmentalism, neopastoralism, and doomerism — disagree fundamentally on growth, technology, and strategy

'Doomer industries' share a pattern: a vague but totalizing disaster scenario, adjacency to commercial opportunity, and a fertile environment for idea generation — which together create a talent gravity well that ordinary cause areas cannot replicate
money_quotes: While the media still portrays climate as a simple question of beliefs, the climate field has long moved on to diversified solutions.
The enemy, in their minds, aren't climate deniers, as we might have seen a decade or two ago — they're other people working in climate.
Climate is better understood not as a singular list of technology and policy action items, but as an assortment of climate tribes.
There is no unified 'we' in climate, any more than there is in the normal world, and pretending otherwise is exactly what keeps us trapped in these failed coordination games.
If religious tendencies cannot be repressed from our collective psyches, why not start with the premise that climate is a religion, then try to understand it on those terms?
They shift the conversation from passive, 'true-believer' narratives towards active, action-oriented ones.
You can't be in a climate tribe without having an opinion about solutions.
Even the end of humanity, it turns out, is a subjective experience.
posts.key_insight: Climate is no longer a monolithic cause defined by belief or disbelief, but a pluralistic landscape of competing tribes whose distinct values and strategies mirror the diversity of the non-climate world — and understanding these tribes is more useful than debating whether climate change is real.

posts.summary: This post introduces a report, supported by Schmidt Futures, analyzing emerging early-stage science funding mechanisms including rapid grants, scout programs, and focused research organizations (FROs). Asparouhova argues that changes in science philanthropy over the past decade have created the beginnings of a 'seed stage' market for science, addressing a critical funding gap for discovery and prototyping. These new mechanisms share common traits: reduced administrative burden, faster cycles, higher risk tolerance, and qualitative evaluation heuristics. While early stage funding has expanded what types of research can be supported—particularly benefiting early-career scientists—the author cautions that grant sizes remain small and long-term impact is unproven.
key_points: A confluence of increased philanthropic capital, shifting PhD career interests, and COVID-19 urgency has created an emerging 'seed stage' market for science funding
Three key mechanisms are emerging: rapid grants (fast turnaround), scout programs (distributed through regrantors), and focused research organizations (time-bounded, goal-oriented entities)
Early stage funders share preferences for reduced administrative burden, faster application cycles, higher risk tolerance, and qualitative evaluation over quantitative metrics
New funding enables previously underfunded work: proofs-of-concept, interdisciplinary research, new field groundwork, and public scientific infrastructure like open-access tools and datasets
Significant limitations remain: small grant sizes (typically under $1M), unknown long-term impact, and a need for greater capital, awareness, and federal adoption
money_quotes: Changes in science philanthropy in the past decade have converged to create the beginnings of a 'seed stage' market for science, which addresses a critical funding gap for early stage discovery and prototyping.
Early stage funders share common interests, including a desire for reduced administrative burden, faster application cycles, and a higher tolerance for risk and failure.
They favor qualitative, rather than quantitative, heuristics to evaluate opportunities and measure impact.

The growth of early stage funding has made it possible to fund more types of research, including proofs-of-concept and prototyping, groundwork for new research fields, interdisciplinary research, and 'public infrastructure' for science.
All of this has been accomplished with fewer administrative costs than is typically required, which suggests there are operational learnings that other funders may want to emulate.
While progress is encouraging so far, early stage funders are not a panacea for all of science funding's problems.
posts.key_insight: An emerging 'seed stage' market in science philanthropy—characterized by rapid grants, scout programs, and focused research organizations—is filling a critical early-stage funding gap, though its long-term impact and scalability remain unproven.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that digital communities and online spaces remain largely unmapped, unlike our physical world where Google Maps has completed centuries of cartographic work. She draws parallels between historical mapmaking and the need to chart digital territories, noting that maps both empower and expose communities. She outlines her methodology for mapping digital worlds—a process of echolocation-like sensing, recording landmarks, and iterative refinement—and surveys various mapping techniques from skeumorphic to algorithmic approaches. She concludes that mapping digital spaces could legitimize them and help reduce misunderstandings when online ideas enter mainstream discourse, even though mapping also creates vulnerability.
key_points: Digital communities remain in an 'IYKYK' unmapped state, causing outsiders to misinterpret them using existing but ill-fitting frameworks, as seen with media coverage of effective altruism

Mapping digital territories is fundamentally different from physical cartography—it requires 'echolocation' and proprioceptive sensing rather than direct observation, and the results are more subjective and ephemeral

The most useful digital maps focus on people and tribes rather than outputs or technologies, because the number of influential people is far smaller and more stable than the constantly changing outputs they produce
Different mapping methodologies (skeumorphic, type-of-guy, schematic, matrix, word cloud) each have distinct tradeoffs between breadth and depth of information conveyed
Mapping digital worlds creates power dynamics similar to physical cartography—it can legitimize communities but also expose them to outside pressure and conquest
money_quotes: We are still in this sort of uncharted, 'natives only,' IYKYK stage of cartography in the digital world.
People who are deep in a particular world often underestimate the value of their own tacit knowledge.
When outsiders do stumble across your digital land, and they don't have a map, they start trying to fit it into the frameworks they do have, and things get very confusing quickly.
Mapping digital territories is more like echolocation, where you're standing in a dark space, totally blind, and you need to 'ping' the space around you and see what you get back.

If there are, say, a million points of output you could be looking at, the number of people creating them is more like a hundred data points. And that's a much easier universe to wrap our heads around.
Maybe digital spaces are still in this ephemeral, nomadic tribe state because we artificially keep them there by refusing to map them.
If we want to take our digital worlds more seriously, part of that starts with finding ways to legitimize them, instead of leaving them undefined.
posts.key_insight: Digital communities remain unmapped and therefore misunderstood by outsiders, and developing rigorous cartographic practices for online spaces could legitimize them and reduce the distortions that occur when digital ideas enter mainstream discourse.

posts.summary: The post examines why the tech industry perceives talent scarcity differently from other industries by proposing three distinct models of talent distribution: normal (process-driven companies like Toyota), Pareto (knowledge-work firms like McKinsey that seek top-percentile 'A-players'), and bimodal (creative and deep-tech companies that depend on irreplaceable 'linchpins'). Asparouhova argues that the '10x developer' concept differs fundamentally from McKinsey's 'top talent' because it describes singular individuals whose abilities can't be replicated, not just high performers on a bell curve. She explains that bimodal distribution is rare but especially relevant in software infrastructure and creative industries, where the possibility space is infinite and linchpins can 'see' solutions others cannot. The framework also accounts for how corporate cultures, management styles, and hiring practices differ across these models, and why software companies often struggle when transitioning from bimodal to Pareto distribution as they mature.
key_points: Three distinct talent distribution models (normal, Pareto, bimodal) explain different corporate cultures and hiring strategies better than a single 'top talent' framework
The '10x developer' concept reflects bimodal distribution—singular linchpins whose abilities are qualitatively unique—rather than Pareto distribution's quantitatively ranked A-players
Software's infinite possibility space and invisible dependencies create a much larger gap between average and exceptional engineers than in physical engineering disciplines
Most modern software companies are actually Pareto-distribution companies selling commoditized products, but the industry's culture still reflects its bimodal origins
Companies' talent distribution needs change over time—Google likely transitioned from bimodal to Pareto as it matured, possibly at the cost of its reputation for innovation
money_quotes: There are only [10-200] people in the world who can do what [highly-paid AI researcher] does. This is a very different statement from, say, 'We are trying to hire top AI researchers.'
Writing software has no such constraints. Solving problems with code has an infinite possibility space; what you can build, and how you build it, is only bound by imagination.

The gap between an average and exceptional software engineer is much bigger than that between an average and exceptional physical engineer, as an exceptional developer can 'see' possibilities that an average one cannot.

While historically, virtually all software companies were tackling difficult technical challenges, today, most probably look more like Pareto-distribution companies, and should follow a different management approach.

If a normal-distribution company is more like socialism – where employees see themselves as part of a bigger machine – the Pareto-distribution company is more like capitalism, where rewards are unevenly distributed, accruing to the best performers.

Silicon Valley skews more qualitative, favors lone 'creative genius' archetypes, and has a positive-sum approach to talent: linchpins could be lurking anywhere, and we surely haven't uncovered them all.
A team comprised of linchpins doesn't necessarily produce great outcomes, versus having teams that are each centered around a linchpin, who's paired with a supporting team.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's notion of talent scarcity reflects a bimodal distribution where irreplaceable 'linchpins' matter more than top-percentile 'A-players,' but most modern software companies have actually shifted to Pareto dynamics without updating their cultural assumptions.

posts.summary: Nadia documents her 18-week journey as a Core Researcher in the Ethereum Foundation-funded Summer of Protocols program, where she investigated protocols as systems of social control. She traces her evolution from struggling to define protocols, through deep research into their material and psychological layers, to producing her final essay 'Dangerous Protocols.' A central tension emerges: protocols are celebrated in tech as enabling customization and interoperability, but historically they exist to standardize and constrain decision-making. She arrives at the insight that protocols, as they become more deeply entrenched, become indistinguishable from our identities, and that the promise of liberation through protocols may itself be an illusion of control.
key_points: Protocols have a fundamental rhetorical disconnect: in tech contexts they promise customization and freedom, but historically they exist to simplify and constrain decision-making, raising questions about whether 'protocolized futures' truly increase human agency
Defining protocols in a nascent field is itself a major challenge — too broad a definition risks meaninglessness, paralleling problems in organizational culture studies and UX
Protocols evolve from explicit to implicit over time, and the most powerful protocols are those we are unaware of, becoming indistinguishable from identity itself

The collaborative research process revealed tensions between synchronous group work and deep individual synthesis, with in-person time proving essential for building shared language in a nascent field

Contemporary internet culture is 'woefully undertheorized' compared to the rich philosophical engagement of the 1990s-2000s, possibly because information overload has numbed our capacity for abstract thinking
money_quotes: Do we really understand what protocols are for, or is this just hopeful rhetoric that distracts us from engaging with more serious problems around the decline of agency today, and its root causes?

I think the notion that protocols are customizable or extensible at all is itself an illusion of control. We like protocols because we think they give us more control, but protocols are actually always in control.

Even when we think we're subverting a protocol, we're often just redirecting the energy but still complying with the rules. If you break out of the protocol entirely, you'd also need to exit the ecosystem that supports it.
A protocolized future is almost certainly highly controlling and constraining — not one where we have a ton of choice.
People had so much to say about the internet in its early days, and then the internet got REALLY weird in the 2010s, and suddenly no one has anything to say about it.
My secret, cynical theory is that we are so completely numbed and overwhelmed by information these days that we simply don't think in these more abstract terms anymore.
It's tempting to add lots of citations to 'show your work,' but ends up making the paper too convoluted and inaccessible and uninteresting.
Kinda felt like being woken up while in the middle of a deep dream.
posts.key_insight: The tech world's enthusiasm for protocols as tools of liberation obscures their historical and fundamental nature as systems of control that constrain agency — and the most powerful protocols are the ones we can no longer see.

posts.summary: Nadia draws parallels between psychedelics, unmedicated childbirth, and jhanas — eight altered states of consciousness accessed through meditation — arguing all are 'exotic states of consciousness' that remain poorly understood by mainstream science. Her personal experience with unmedicated birth revealed how psychology influences physical outcomes, which opened her mind to jhana practitioners' claims of achieving MDMA- or LSD-like states through concentration alone. Despite deep skepticism, she attended a Jhourney retreat and within hours experienced profound altered states using only her mind. She highlights that only three notable academic studies on jhanas exist, and calls for more secular research into a phenomenon that could have significant therapeutic implications.
key_points: Childbirth, psychedelics, and jhanas are all 'exotic states of consciousness' where psychology profoundly influences subjective and even medical outcomes
The jhanas are a series of eight altered states accessible through meditation that practitioners claim rival MDMA or psychedelic experiences — without substances
Despite personal skepticism and no meditation background, Nadia experienced MDMA-like, out-of-body, and psychedelic-level states within 15 hours of jhana practice
Only three notable academic studies on jhanas have been published in the last decade, and secular language to describe the phenomenon is conspicuously absent
The jhanas remain trapped in meditation subculture rather than being studied as a form of behavioral therapy, echoing how psychedelics and birth psychology were long dismissed
money_quotes: Because birth manifests so clearly in the body, I had assumed it was something that happened to me – like getting the flu, or arthritis – rather than something could be influenced, to some degree, by my mind.

Both these 'exotic states of consciousness' suggest that there are still vast oceans to explore inside our brains, which influence not just fuzzy, hard-to-measure things like mood or motivation, but in some cases – as with birth – vastly different medical outcomes.

It took decades for researchers to understand how psychedelics worked: to differentiate them from chemically-induced psychosis, to identify that they had therapeutic benefits, and to be able to discuss them openly.
I am not a meditator. Nor am I a 'spiritual seeker' of the sort you might find at Burning Man or a Vipassana retreat.

Within my first hour of practice, I found myself in the equivalent of an MDMA roll. With ten hours of practice, I had an out-of-body experience. Less than fifteen hours, a psychedelic experience that rivaled what might be had on LSD. All of this took place using only concentration.
I left astonished, and perplexed, as to why barely anyone has studied the jhanas at all.
Strangest to me, as an outsider, is that the jhanas are still firmly lodged in meditation circles and norms, rather than studied as a form of behavioral therapy.
If people can access these experiences with relatively little effort – and to do so legally, for free – more ought to know that such a thing exists.
posts.key_insight: The jhanas represent a profound and accessible altered state of consciousness that mainstream science has barely studied, echoing the decades-long lag in understanding psychedelics and the psychology of childbirth.

posts.summary: Asparouhova traces her journey from exploring crypto and open source governance to a broader investigation of protocols as systems of control. She recounts an early, unanswered question about decentralized file hosting governance that revealed the tension between protocol liberation and accountability. She argues that protocolization has expanded far beyond technology to shape how we think, act, and prioritize attention, drawing a parallel between 1930s bureaucracy simplifying Industrial Age information and today's crisis mindset simplifying the Digital Age's opinion boom. She frames LLMs as the natural output of this compression impulse. The post introduces her essay on 'dangerous protocols,' written for the Ethereum Foundation's Summer of Protocols research group, which examines how protocols usurp agency and how we might subvert them.
key_points: Decentralization creates accountability vacuums: most people prefer one identifiable adversary over a thousand faceless ones
Protocolization has expanded beyond technology to encompass thought, behavior, dating, opinions, and intellectual pursuits
Today's crisis mindset is Protocolization 2.0 — an attempt to simplify the explosion of opinions in the Digital Age, just as bureaucracy simplified Industrial Age information
LLMs are the expected output of this era: an attempt to compress human creativity into something predictable
Despite its downsides, protocolization can propel humanity forward, but requires conscious effort to preserve human agency
money_quotes: Most people would rather have one identifiable adversary to grapple with, instead of a thousand faceless ones with middling levels of motivation: or worse, not know who their adversary is at all.
It seemed that everything – how we thought, how we acted, who we dated, the opinions we held, even our intellectual pursuits – had become protocolized, and always with a tinge of urgency.
LLMs are the expected output of this era: an attempt to compress human creativity into something predictable.
Today's crisis mindset is an attempt to simplify a boom of opinions that have proliferated in the Digital Age.
If everything is running on autopilot now, it's especially critical for us to swim into the machine and identify the kernel of that-which-makes-us-human that enables us to steer the ship.
Understanding protocols, then, was a way to understand our present cultural moment.
posts.key_insight: Protocolization has expanded from technical systems to cultural life itself, compressing human agency and creativity into predictable patterns — and recognizing this is the first step toward reclaiming autonomy.

posts.summary: Nadia provides a practical, demystified guide to achieving the jhanas — eight progressive altered mental states culminating in cessation (loss of consciousness) — which she learned in roughly 20 hours across two retreats despite minimal prior meditation experience. She frames jhanas as a body-encoded technology akin to software, arguing that the Buddhist theoretical scaffolding surrounding them unnecessarily gatekeeps what is essentially a learnable skill. She details her specific techniques for each stage, from inducing euphoria through sustained attention on joy, to navigating the dissociative and reality-dissolving higher states. Ultimately, she argues that the jhanas themselves are not the point — they are an algorithm for arriving at fundamental insights about consciousness, after which the practice feels genuinely complete.
key_points: Jhanas are a learnable technology encoded in our bodies, not an esoteric Buddhist practice requiring years of meditation experience — Nadia progressed through all nine states in about 20 hours
The key mechanism is sustained, concentrated attention acting as an amplifier on positive emotions, essentially inducing 'the opposite of a panic attack'

The jhanas progress from euphoria (J1) through contentment and peace (J2-J4), then into dissociative states dissolving space, consciousness, and perception (J5-J8), culminating in cessation of consciousness (J9)
Jhanas represent a form of 'natural psychedelics' that could offer therapeutic benefits without chemical interventions or regulatory barriers

The real value lies not in the altered states themselves but in the insights gained — particularly after cessation, which prompted philosophical reflections that left the practice feeling genuinely complete
money_quotes: Jhanas are an algorithm in the oldest sense of the word: a set of instructions that, if executed correctly, solve for a problem that you may not have even realized you've been trying to unravel.
They are an Easter egg hiding in the game of life.

If you had recently figured out how to code, and realized it was really quite simple and teachable to others – then looked around, and all you saw were computer scientists warning off would-be developers from making software – wouldn't that make you want to open up a text editor and type out your own version of things?
Jhanas are the closest thing to magic that I've experienced in my adult life.
I don't think the jhanas made me happy, but their biggest impact was enabling me to realize how happy I already was.
The jhanas are useful for tinkering with the mind, but after cessation, I realized that neither body nor mind is really all that important.
How often does mastery of an activity end with true fulfillment, rather than boredom, distraction, or disinterest?
One day, we might look back on psychedelics as an early, coarse attempt to do this sort of thing that came with all sorts of weird side effects, like dentists using cocaine in the late 1800s.
posts.key_insight: Jhanas are a learnable, body-encoded technology for systematically intensifying positive mental states through concentrated attention, but their ultimate purpose is not the altered states themselves — it is the fundamental insights about consciousness that emerge from completing the full progression through cessation.

posts.summary: This post investigates whether prior meditation experience predicts success with jhanas, a series of altered mental states accessed through concentration. Analyzing data from 81 participants across Jhourney retreats, the author finds virtually no difference in jhana success rates between experienced and beginner meditators. While experienced meditators showed some tendency to progress further when successful, these differences were not statistically attributable to meditation experience itself. The author hypothesizes that jhana success depends on two underlying skills — the ability to invoke positive feelings and the ability to sustain attention — rather than meditation hours logged. She draws an analogy to Rich Sutton's 'bitter lesson' in AI, suggesting that brute-force practice of core skills may matter more than introspective understanding of one's mental barriers.
key_points: Data from 81 retreat participants shows no significant difference in jhana success rates between experienced and beginner meditators, regardless of how experience is measured

While experienced meditators showed some tendency to progress further through jhanic states, correlation analysis revealed no significant link between any measure of meditation experience and jhana skill
The author proposes that jhana success depends on two specific skills: the ability to invoke a positive feeling in the body and the ability to sustain attention
Popular meditation practices like mindfulness or Vipassana may develop mechanistically different skills than those needed for jhana access
Drawing on Sutton's 'bitter lesson' from AI, the author suggests brute-force cultivation of joy and attention may be more effective than deep introspection into psychological barriers
money_quotes: Across all three dimensions of meditation experience, we see virtually no difference in success rates between experienced vs. beginner meditators.
We found no correlation between meditation experience and any jhana skill.
We ought to exercise caution in describing jhanas as an 'advanced meditation practice,' because it could deter those who might otherwise succeed – and personally benefit – from the jhanas.

What does it mean to be an 'experienced' meditator, in the way that someone is an 'experienced' swimmer? Number of hours meditated tells us how long someone has been practicing, but not how adept they are at cultivating and sustaining a quiet mind.
Experienced meditators may have more confidence that they know what meditation is, so while they have assets, they are also at times mistaken about which skills to use and when.
Someone who practices violin for an hour a day is not necessarily proficient at the violin.

Instead of introspecting heavily – trying to model where these feelings came from, and how they impact one's behavior – it could be more effective to simply 'brute force' one's way into sparking joy and cultivating attention.
posts.key_insight: Jhana success appears driven not by accumulated meditation experience but by two specific underlying skills — invoking positive feelings and sustaining attention — suggesting we should measure aptitude rather than practice hours.

posts.summary: Asparouhova continues exploring jhanas—altered states of consciousness accessible through concentration—after attending meditation retreats that profoundly changed her perspective. She wrote pragmatic, non-spiritual instructions for reaching jhana states that proved successful for newcomers. Partnering with the retreat company Jhourney, she investigated whether meditation experience correlates with jhana success and found no correlation. This finding leads her to question whether jhanas should even be classified as a 'meditation practice' and whether meditation itself is being confused with the deeper skills it aims to develop.
key_points: Asparouhova progressed through all eight or nine jhanic states in roughly 20 cumulative hours, far faster than the months or years traditionally expected
An analysis of anonymized meditator data found no correlation between meditation experience and success with jhanas, challenging conventional assumptions
The finding raises fundamental questions about what meditation experience actually measures and whether meditation is a means or an end
She hypothesizes that spending her days in focused creative states through writing and research may have provided the underlying attentional skills that meditation typically develops
money_quotes: I went back to try it again, and accidentally discovered how to voluntarily turn my consciousness on and off, which occasioned an experience so profound that I decided not to write about it.
We did not find any correlation between meditation experience and success with the jhanas.
This finding casts into question why jhanas are described as a 'meditation practice' at all.
I think meditation is a finger, and we don't know what the moon is yet.
Someone who meditates every day is not necessarily adept at that skill. They're just someone who meditates often.
We shouldn't conflate 'meditation experience' with the underlying skills it develops.
Even though I don't formally meditate, I am very lucky to spend pretty much all day in a focused, creative state, thanks to the nature of my work.
posts.key_insight: Meditation may be merely one method—a finger pointing at the moon—for developing underlying attentional skills, and we should stop conflating the practice itself with the deeper capacities it aims to cultivate.

posts.summary: Asparouhova expresses frustration at the tech industry's defensive reaction to Jonathan Haidt's 'The Anxious Generation,' which examines harmful effects of phone and social media use on young people. She argues that the destruction of attention through smartphones and social media is one of civilization's biggest threats, compromising our ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile. She wrote a piece for Arena magazine exploring why tech is psychologically avoidant of the social media debate. She contends that tech's defensiveness stems from historical and psychological scars rather than genuine disagreement with the obvious harms of doomscrolling. Her core plea is that the tech industry should demonstrate leadership in helping people live peaceably with powerful technology rather than dismissing legitimate concerns as Luddism.
key_points: The tech industry's hostile reaction to Haidt's book reflects cognitive dissonance rather than genuine disagreement, since nobody actually feels good after hours of doomscrolling
The destruction of attention through smartphones and social media is one of civilization's biggest threats because it compromises the ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile
Engaging with social media criticism risks being labeled anti-tech, which keeps many in tech from acknowledging plainly obvious harms
The conversation about social media's effects is still in its early stages despite being 20+ years into the phenomenon, and tech has an opportunity to demonstrate leadership
money_quotes: Nobody feels good after scrolling on their phones for hours, and this simple observation alone seems to reasonably justify a deeper examination of social media's effects on our psychology.

The destruction of our attention – thanks, in large part, to the combination of smartphones and social media – is one of the biggest threats we face as a civilization, as it compromises our ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile.
Most people are so immersed in it that they can't see how bad it is; you can't ask someone to think clearly when their core operating system is compromised.
Running on a hamster wheel is not the same as running a marathon.
I care about this topic because I love tech and all that it hopes to promise the world: a better future for everyone.

We need to look beyond the words being said, and instead understand the motivations – and scars – that make it difficult for many people in tech to acknowledge a thing that everyone else seems to find plainly obvious.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's refusal to seriously engage with social media's harms stems not from genuine disagreement but from psychological defensiveness, and this avoidance itself is a threat to civilization's ability to make meaningful progress.

posts.summary: Asparouhova reports on her new research into jhanas, a series of intensely altered meditation states she has been covering since early 2024. She traces the rapid shift in public discourse from skepticism about whether jhanas are real to widespread curiosity about how to practice them. Partnering with Jhourney, a retreat company with unique access to practitioners, she surveyed alumni about how jhanas changed their lives. The resulting report is described as the most extensive study yet on jhana impact, aimed at identifying the right research questions rather than providing definitive answers. She notes the research challenges: few academics studying jhanas, difficulty recruiting participants, and unclear frameworks for measuring impact given jhanas sit between meditation and psychedelics.
key_points: Public discourse on jhanas shifted rapidly in 2024 from skepticism about their existence to widespread curiosity about practicing them
Jhanas occupy a difficult research space between meditation and psychedelics, making it unclear what type of impact to measure
The report, produced with retreat company Jhourney, is the most extensive survey yet on how jhanas affect practitioners' lives
Academic research on jhanas is severely limited by few researchers, slow institutional pace, and difficulty recruiting skilled participants
The goal was not definitive answers but identifying the right questions, which is already informing a follow-up academic collaboration
money_quotes: Instead of debating whether they are real, the question I hear now is: 'How can I try it?'
Jhanas feel like self-induced psychedelic states, so – do they offer similar benefits?
Because jhanas occupy a weird space between 'meditation' and 'psychedelics,' it's not even clear which type of impact to look for, much less measure.
Mindfulness and MDMA might both change your outlook, but in very different ways.
A big, open research question with little prior art to draw from? Sounds fun!
My initial goal was helping us figure out the right questions to even ask.
posts.key_insight: Jhanas have rapidly moved from fringe skepticism to mainstream curiosity, but the research infrastructure to understand their impact barely exists, creating an open frontier that requires figuring out the right questions before seeking answers.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova announces her new book 'Antimemetics: Why Some Ideas Resist Spreading,' inspired by qntm's science fiction novel 'There Is No Antimemetics Division.' She defines antimemes as self-censoring ideas including taboos, cognitive biases, and memory lapses that resist being shared despite their importance. The book examines how the social web is shifting from public virality toward private and semi-private spaces like group chats and DMs, where the most interesting ideas now circulate. She frames this as a nonfiction treatment of antimemetics grounded in real-world phenomena, connecting it to her previous work on open source sustainability. The book is published through the Dark Forest Collective, which she sees as fitting given that antimemes would be crushed by conventional publishing's virality-driven approach. She positions the work as a sequel of sorts to 'Working in Public,' arguing that the withdrawal from public spaces she observed among open source developers has now become universal.
key_points: Antimemes are a broad category of self-censoring ideas—taboos, cognitive biases, memory lapses—that resist being remembered or shared despite their importance
The social web is shifting from public virality toward private spaces: group chats, invite-only newsletters, and private apps now carry the most interesting ideas
Group chats are inherently antimemetic—we can only know what we've experienced ourselves, making them nearly impossible to write about publicly
Many people feel conflicted between wanting to withdraw from the chaos of public social media and feeling guilty about disengaging from public discourse
The pattern Asparouhova observed among open source developers retreating from public attention in 'Working in Public' has now become a broader societal phenomenon
money_quotes: It's easier than ever to share ideas. But in recent years, some of the most interesting ideas are burrowing deeper underground, circulating quietly among group chats and texts instead of public social channels.

Memes may dominate how we communicate online, but in the last few years, their rapid spread has also necessitated the birth of a parallel, shadow ecosystem, where the best ideas are paradoxically harder to find.
Chasing virality is no longer a sufficient motive to explain why we do (or don't) share what we do online. But we still don't have the vocabulary to describe what we're doing instead.
It's much more comfortable to live in our group chats and DMs, but doesn't it feel a little strange to watch the world burn from the safety of our miniaturized abodes?

The past is the past: we need to let it go. Instead, I wanted to take a hard, unflinching look at where we are today, and try to figure out how to make meaning from our lives with the constraints we currently have.

Antimemes are delicate and messy and hard to look at; they would be swiftly crushed in the hands of a publisher trying to juice it up and make it 'go viral,' like a flower ground under the heel of a shoe.

I wrote that book partly because I felt that what was happening to open source developers – who, in the face of overwhelming demand for their attention, were withdrawing from public online spaces – was going to happen to all of us.
posts.key_insight: The rapid spread of memes has paradoxically created a shadow ecosystem of antimemes—ideas that resist sharing—driving the most interesting discourse underground into private spaces, and we lack the vocabulary to understand this shift.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova provides a roundup of her recent work across three seemingly disparate areas: her book Antimemetics (which argues against both left and right narratives about internet information), her advanced meditation research with Harvard's Meditation Research Program, and an essay on Silicon Valley's political ideology for American Affairs. She frames these varied pursuits through the metaphor of a Codenames game, acknowledging the challenge of connecting them into a coherent narrative. The meditation work focuses on making contemplative practice measurable and trainable, while the political essay argues Silicon Valley defies conventional ideological labels. The post is self-aware about its scattered nature, inviting readers to engage with whichever thread interests them.
key_points: Her book Antimemetics proposes a third way beyond both left-wing information gatekeeping and right-wing mimetic desire frameworks for understanding internet discourse
She is collaborating with Harvard's Meditation Research Program to develop education initiatives that translate scientific and contemplative knowledge into actionable insights for ordinary people

Research from her work with Jhourney found that people who overestimate their abilities are less likely to access deeper meditation states, and that meditation experience (hours practiced) doesn't correlate well with actual abilities

Her American Affairs essay argues Silicon Valley is not libertarian, was never classically liberal, and is not undergoing a rightward shift — it is its own undefinable political entity that chronically lacks allies
She frames meditation as a trainable, measurable skill, pushing back against the meditation community's resistance to rigorous assessment
money_quotes: She does not think that all communication can be reduced to a power struggle, she is not ready to give up on democratic values or civilization tout court, and she considers herself one of many 'refugees fleeing memetic contagion.'
As you might imagine, it's not easy to find people like this in meditation land, where many conversations quickly unravel into Buddhist poetry and start to feel more like a hazing ritual.
People who overestimate their abilities are less likely to access deeper states.
Meditation experience (as defined by e.g. number of hours meditated) doesn't seem to correlate to abilities, so I think there's room for more experimentation here.
Silicon Valley is not libertarian. Nor was it ever classically liberal. Nor is it currently undergoing a 'rightward shift.'

Tech is its own, strange, undefinable thing that chronically suffers from a lack of allies, as evinced by its getting dumped by the Democratic Party in the 2010s, and its current, growing dissatisfaction with the Republican Party in the 2020s.
I hope it helps address some of the outdated notions that tech's behavior can be attributed to merely being libertarian, obsessively capitalist, or autistically engineer-brained.
Many meditators push back on the idea that their skills can be measured or assessed in any rigorous way.
posts.key_insight: Across internet culture, meditation, and tech politics, Asparouhova consistently seeks frameworks that resist easy categorization and insists on rigorous, empirical approaches to domains typically governed by ideology or mysticism.

posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that frontier biotech companies like Loyal, Orchid, and Until have succeeded not by engaging in memetic warfare but by making their ideas relatable, concrete, and credible. She contrasts this with the conventional internet playbook of polarization and coded language, noting that antimemetic ideas — those too consequential to comfortably acknowledge — require a gentler approach. The essay examines how reframing language (e.g., 'anti-aging' to 'extending healthy lifespan') and choosing approachable entry points (longevity for dogs, screening for health conditions) helped normalize previously taboo fields. She then applies these lessons to contemplative science, suggesting sleep could be a 'killer app' that concretizes vague promises of wellbeing. The broader argument is that when technologies touch intimate aspects of people's lives, trust-building outperforms tribalistic point-scoring.
key_points: Frontier biotech founders have succeeded by explicitly rejecting the memetic warfare playbook of polarization and coded language, instead building trust through credibility and approachability.
Antimemetic ideas — those too consequential to comfortably process — require a communication strategy centered on being relatable, concrete, and credible rather than virally provocative.
Strategic reframing of language (anti-aging to 'extending healthy lifespan,' designer babies to 'have healthy babies') has been instrumental in normalizing previously taboo scientific fields.
Two failure modes exist: being too vague about outcomes (promising 'happiness') or overcorrecting toward scientific credibility at the expense of relatability (fMRI scans without practical context).
Sleep could serve as a concrete 'killer app' for contemplative science, much as GLP-1s entered through weight loss but showed broader therapeutic promise.
money_quotes: Memes and mimicry might explain some of our behavior today, but they aren't an excuse to roll over and accept things exactly as they are.

If one were to follow the conventional internet playbook today, the best way to address such concerns is to own it. Take a stance. Don't be afraid to step on people's toes. Use polarizing, coded language to strengthen your support base.

When trying to spread an idea that touches people's lives, families, and personal values in an intimate way, leaning into memetic contagion can make them feel more alienated and afraid, as if the idea is running away faster than they can safely process it.
This is classically antimemetic behavior, where an idea feels too consequential to acknowledge.
By explicitly taking on the role of the skeptic, one can build a rapport with readers who might have otherwise tuned out the message.
The other, and perhaps less obvious, failure mode I've seen is overcorrecting for credibility and concreteness at the expense of relatability.
When the public expresses fear of technologies like social media or AI, tech often reacts by doubling down on their ingroup, instead of trying to genuinely engage with these concerns.

If the goal is to make exciting new technologies socially legible, rather than scoring cheap points for one's team, it's worth trying a playbook that communicates from a place of trust and reassurance to win public approval.
posts.key_insight: Ideas that are too consequential for people to comfortably process require trust-building through relatability, concreteness, and credibility — not the viral polarization of memetic warfare.

posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova examines the practical limitations of embryo selection technologies, arguing that media discourse overstates their current capabilities. She identifies three key constraints: IVF produces too few embryos (typically 3-5) for meaningful trait selection beyond essential disease screening, polygenic screening is statistically unlikely to reveal genetic outliers from such small samples, and polygenic risk scores themselves are unreliable models with wide error bars that vary across providers. While acknowledging the genuine value of genetic testing for serious monogenic diseases, she urges transparency about uncertainty and caution before making life-altering decisions based on speculative polygenic data. She concludes that the underlying goal of preventing genetic disease remains worthwhile, whether achieved through embryo selection, gene editing, or future technologies.
key_points: A single IVF cycle yields only 3-5 embryos, and after filtering for quality grade, sex, and even one genetic trait, viable options shrink dramatically—making 'designer baby' shopping among many traits practically impossible today.
Polygenic screening produces results clustered near the middle of a bell curve for any two parents, meaning with only a handful of embryos you're unlikely to find a meaningful genetic outlier.
Polygenic risk scores are statistical models, not hard-coded truths—they vary by provider, are often trained only on European ancestry data, and can give conflicting results for the same person.
Most current embryo screening is used for its clearest application: avoiding passing severe monogenic diseases like Huntington's or cystic fibrosis to children.
The broader ambition of preventing genetic disease may ultimately be better served by gene editing than embryo selection, bypassing the embryo quantity constraint entirely.
money_quotes: A world in which parents are 'shopping' among lots of different traits in their embryos simply doesn't exist today.
Finding a genetic outlier is like sticking your hand in a jar of green jelly beans and rummaging blindly around, hoping to grab the one red jelly bean: it's mostly wishful thinking.
Genomics researchers can't just look at our DNA, like some soothsayer blowing the dust off an ancient text, and tell us what they see.
A polygenic risk score is just a model that captures our best-guess thinking at the time. Like any model, it can have wide error bars.
It's another story to make life-altering choices for ourselves, or our children, based on highly speculative data, when the default choice might have been just fine.
If these services can't help customers understand what they should actually do differently, having access to these data insights might be more counterproductive than not having them at all.
Despite the panic about designer babies, most embryo screening today is being used for exactly this: avoiding passing severe, life-altering diseases on to one's children.
posts.key_insight: Embryo selection is far more constrained than popular discourse suggests—limited embryo counts, statistical clustering, and unreliable polygenic models mean the technology is practically useful only for screening serious monogenic diseases, not designing children.

posts.summary: This is Nadia Asparouhova's projects page, which serves as a portfolio overview of her major research areas. She outlines four main threads of work: advanced meditation and altered mental states, Silicon Valley's growing political and social influence, open source software sustainability and the creator economy parallel, and experimental philanthropy through microgrants. Each project reflects her approach as an independent researcher who documents her process publicly to encourage others.
key_points: She is exploring advanced meditation practices that can induce psychedelic-comparable altered states through concentration alone, with potential therapeutic benefits
Her tech culture research examines how Silicon Valley shifted from building startups to building movements and engaging with public institutions in the early 2020s
Her open source work evolved from arguing that open source is public infrastructure requiring maintenance to documenting how modern open source parallels the creator economy
She ran Helium Grants, a two-year microgrants experiment funding individuals rather than organizations with no-strings-attached grants of $1K-$5K
money_quotes: Open source code is a form of public infrastructure that requires maintenance.
Tech had shifted from primarily building and funding startups to building movements and engaging with public institutions.
I wanted to understand why this was happening from a systems perspective; key events that drove this change; and how tech's story rhymes with prior historical industry wealth booms.
The experience of pursuing this topic across a few different gigs also gave me a blueprint for understanding myself as an independent researcher.
I wanted to experiment with faster grantmaking processes, as well as funding individuals rather than organizations.
posts.key_insight: Asparouhova's research portfolio reveals a consistent thread of examining how decentralized, individual-driven phenomena — from open source maintenance to independent philanthropy to tech's political awakening — reshape public institutions and infrastructure.
